summary.overall: Chapter 10 was reviewed in 13 bounded source-grounded shards. Proposed changes: 223 replacements, 2 splits, and 8 removals.
patterns: Paragraph-location scaffolding in 100% of question stems (11 of 11) and in the explanations of 10 of 11 questions; the stems open with 'Under 10-1-X' or 'Under the note to 10-1-2' rather than standing as self-contained operational prompts.

Thin explanations in all three true/false items that only cite the paragraph number (e.g., 'That instruction is stated in 10-1-1d' or 'That note is stated in 10-1-2') rather than teaching why the statement is correct.

One activity format mismatch: a readback_check presents a pilot Mayday declaration as the 'clearance' field and asks which readback is correct, but controllers do not read back pilot distress declarations.

One flashcard back combines the rule from 10-1-1c (handle as emergency when Mayday/Pan-Pan not used) with the rule from 10-1-1d (choose most appropriate course of action), diluting the retrieval target.

Strengths: all distractors represent plausible operational errors; situation_action activities provide rich operational context with meaningful trade-offs; correct answers are factually accurate to source; the three knowledge_check activities are well-constructed with substantive explanations.
Universal paragraph-location scaffolding: every one of 13 question stems opens with 'Under 10-1-X,' training students to associate answers with paragraph numbers rather than operational situations.

Quote-recognition true/false: four items (554bac7a, 03c74e70, c907e6ec, ec68c145) present the paragraph text verbatim as a true/false stem and explain the answer solely by citing the paragraph location. These test source memory rather than ATC decision-making.
Thin explanations: most explanations restate the correct answer without teaching the condition, exception, or operational context that distinguishes the correct answer from the distractors.

Activity quality is notably higher: only two of five activities use paragraph-location scaffolding (a51bc54a and 3726bfc4 in their para_context fields). The three knowledge_check activities are well-constructed.

Flashcard quality is generally good: three of four flashcards are correctly typed as procedure cards with clean front/back alignment. One flashcard (03827d28) has a front-back mismatch (front asks 'who' but back answers as a procedure description) and is mistyped as definition.
The short paragraph 10-1-5 (a single sentence) carries three questions, two activities, and one flashcard — a high item-to-content ratio that strains retrieval diversity.
10-1-6's note about aircraft proximity to emergency locations (wake turbulence, congestion, distraction hazards) has no learning items despite containing operational risks worth teaching.
Paragraph-location scaffolding is the overwhelming defect — 18 of 22 items reference paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or context fields, violating the self-contained context standard.
Explanations are factually accurate but four items provide only thin restatements or paragraph citations instead of teaching the operational principle and its scope.
All answers are source-faithful; no incorrect answer keys or misleading distractors were found across any format.
The three flashcards are clean: concise fronts, focused backs, no scaffolding, appropriate rote targets for prescribed minima (minimum emergency info, ELT test window, bomb-threat prohibition).

Cross-format reinforcement exists for the 'do not suggest bomb-handling actions' prohibition (question, activity, flashcard) and minimum emergency information (question, activity, flashcard) — appropriately varied retrieval modes.
Automated negative_stem flags on situation_action activities are false positives; 'What should you do?' is not a negative stem.
No missing coverage gaps identified within the bounded source blocks; the essential operational duties, notifications, and limitations are represented across formats.

Paragraph-location scaffolding: 10 of 11 question stems reference paragraph numbers. Both situation_action para_context fields open with 'Under 10-2-XX.' Most explanations cite paragraph numbers (e.g., 'Per 10-2-12c') rather than teaching the operational rule.

Thin explanations: Many explanations restate the correct answer in different words without teaching condition, exception, or operational context. The T/F explanations are particularly thin — e.g., 'That direction appears in 10-2-13d.'
T/F stem format: Two true/false questions (15c856a3, eb7bdbee) use interrogative stems ('what should ATC do?') instead of evaluable propositions. This obscures the T/F format.

Cross-format coverage is adequate within each paragraph — each concept appears as at least a question and an activity. Phraseology paragraphs (10-2-13, 10-2-14) include readback_check and spot_the_error activities which are effective for prescribed wording.
All distractors are defensible operational errors — swapping broadcast intervals, confusing similar procedures, or omitting required elements. No implausible or giveaway distractors.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 22 of 24 items include paragraph references in stems, explanations, or both, teaching students to associate rules with section numbers rather than operational contexts.

Thin explanations in true/false items: several T/F explanations simply state 'That [requirement/direction] appears in [paragraph]' without teaching the underlying rule or its operational significance.
Answer-length cues in EOVM/EAM items: correct answers for cfc6c4bf, ee3d4647, and ce48b519 are substantially longer than their distractors, creating an unintended test-taking cue.

One flashcard invents source content: ee76f1f4's back lists 'rescue services,' 'length and surface,' and 'any other factor' — none of which appear in the 10-2-15 source factors — while omitting aircraft type, pilot qualifications, and vectoring/homing capability.
Situation-action activities are a consistent strength: realistic controller scenarios with plausible peer-pressure distractors that test integrated knowledge of multiple factors.
Distractors are generally plausible operational mistakes rather than obviously wrong answers, maintaining appropriate difficulty.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 14 of 19 items include paragraph references ('Under 10-2-19', 'Per 10-2-2', 'Under 10-2-3') in stems, explanations, or both, teaching students to associate rules with section numbers rather than the operational task.
Distractor 'Only' prefix cue: two 10-2-19 items (fa1f98c4, 9ed97a7c) use 'Only...' to introduce every distractor while the correct answer lacks that prefix, creating a systematic answer-wording cue.

Thin explanations in T/F items: the original T/F explanations for aa1830b7, 082436a3, 18c3a702, and 3da29436 merely cite where in the order a rule appears rather than teaching the controlling operational rule.

T/F redundancy with MCQs: the T/F questions 18c3a702 and 3da29436 test the same concepts as existing MCQs (0fb2d978 and fc298864 respectively) in the same retrieval mode, contributing no additional assessment value.
One flashcard back is underspecified: 34d1b5d2 says 'Obtain the listed information' without specifying what information — a document-reference placeholder instead of teachable content.
Situation-action activities are a consistent strength: realistic controller scenarios with plausible peer-pressure distractors that test integrated knowledge of procedural duties.
Distractors are generally plausible operational mistakes rather than obviously wrong answers.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 20 of 24 items include paragraph references ('Under 10-2-4', 'Per 10-2-5', 'Under 10-2-7') in stems, explanations, or activity prompts, teaching students to associate rules with section numbers rather than the operational task.

Two source-structure errors: question c949008e cites a non-existent '10-2-7c' sub-paragraph; question 8fe4f770 references a 'note under 10-2-7' that does not exist in the source blocks. The source only has 10-2-7a and 10-2-7b in the body text.

Thin explanations in all true/false items: the three T/F explanations (1ee86295, 1243ed5a, 528f6809) merely confirm correctness or cite paragraph numbers without teaching the underlying operational principle.
All four flashcards are well-constructed: specific fronts, concise backs appropriate for single-retrieval-target recall, no paragraph references, no format defects.

The hijack verification phraseology (VERIFY SQUAWKING SEVEN FIVE ZERO ZERO) is the best-reinforced concept in the shard, appearing in an MCQ, a readback_check activity, and a spot_the_error activity — providing diverse retrieval practice.

Situation-action activities are effective at integrating multiple procedural requirements (e.g., 96c93363 combines the altitude recommendation rule with the volcanic ash caution in a realistic peer-pressure scenario).
One activity (eebced63) has a correct answer noticeably longer than its distractors, though the scenario structure partially explains the difference.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 15 of 20 items include paragraph references in stems, explanations, or activity prompts. The generation process systematically prepended 'Under [para_id]' to question stems and 'Per [para_id]' to explanations.

Thin explanations in three true/false items: the T/F questions (ee162594, 705e2915, 1dbdcb44) and one activity (9ca00672) provide explanations that merely cite paragraph numbers or confirm correctness rather than teaching the operational rule, threshold, or condition.

One incorrect paragraph-reference label: question ee162594 cites '10-2-8b2' in its explanation, but the source organizes this content under 10-2-8c2(b). The generation process hallucinated a sub-paragraph structure.

Two items with answer-length cues: activity 578ee2aa (10-2-8) and question 72d8a680 (10-3-1) have correct answers noticeably longer than distractors, creating a test-taking cue independent of content knowledge.

All three flashcards are well-constructed: specific fronts, concise backs appropriate for single-retrieval-target recall, no paragraph references, no format defects. The flashcard generation process appears to follow different (better) prompting than question generation.

The situation-action activities (e6d3d6bf, aa581ed0) are effective at integrating multiple procedural requirements in realistic peer-pressure scenarios. Once paragraph-location scaffolding is removed from their para_context and explanation fields, they are strong items.

Two 10-2-9 activities (23b1ada9 and 4a7a6127) are correctly constructed with clean stems, good distractors, and operational explanations, demonstrating that the generation process can produce adequate items when it avoids paragraph-number scaffolding.

Several techniques from the 10-2-9 list (a-f) receive cross-format coverage: climb/descent in turn avoidance appears in a question, activity, and situation-action activity; vectoring to VFR appears in a T/F and list_membership activity; gear/airspeed configuration appears in a knowledge_check activity and flashcard.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 17 of 22 items embed 'Under 10-3-X' or 'Per 10-3-X' in stems, explanations, or activity context, teaching document navigation instead of operational decision-making.
Answer-length cues: two multiple-choice questions have correct answers substantially longer than distractors, revealing the answer by text volume alone.
Thin true/false items: four true/false questions restate source statements as 'True' answers, testing recognition rather than application of the rule.
Weak distractors: several multiple-choice questions use distractors beginning with 'Only,' which are implausibly narrow and easily eliminated.

Vague flashcard backs: the flashcard for 10-3-2 uses generic groupings ('last communication and position details') instead of listing the specific information items, and the 10-3-3 flashcard uses 'detailed aircraft data' as a placeholder for a 13-item list.
Overloaded flashcards: both 10-3-2 and 10-3-3 flashcards attempt to cover 11-13 information items in a single card, producing either vagueness or excessive length.

Duplicate concept: the note about non-FSS facilities referring inquiries to FSS appears identically in both 10-3-2 and 10-3-3, and both paragraphs test it as a question — once as multiple choice and once as true/false.
Paragraph-location explanations: many explanations use 'Per 10-3-X' as their only substantive content, failing to teach the controlling operational principle.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is universal: every question stem begins with 'Under 10-X-Y' and every explanation contains 'Per 10-X-Y' or similar. This teaches paragraph-number trivia, not operational recall.
Three true_false items have thin explanations that state only where the instruction appears ('That instruction appears in 10-3-6c') rather than describing the operational purpose or context.
One activity's correct answer runs ~270 characters while distractors run ~125–150, creating a length cue. The compound procedure makes brevity difficult, but tightening is possible.

Two items carry automated negative_stem flags; one (question 1f2be6ad) is reframed to test the complete rule including the radar-separation exception, while the other (activity 53d34579) uses the negative condition legitimately as scenario framing.
No items are redundant within the shard; each question targets a distinct subparagraph or operational distinction.

Paragraph-location scaffolding in 19 of 24 items — stems, explanations, and activity contexts introduce the paragraph number before teaching the operational rule, which encourages rote document-structure recall over applied decision-making.
Explanations tend to restate the answer rather than teach the controlling condition, exception, or operational principle, though they avoid inventing unsupported safety rationales.
Answer-length cues in two multiple-choice questions where the correct answer is a compound procedural list while distractors are short phrases, making the answer identifiable by length alone.
Single-sentence paragraph 10-6-1 is over-targeted with two questions (MCQ and T/F), one activity, and one flashcard — the T/F duplicates the MCQ's test of the same fact.
All five flashcards are concise, procedurally accurate, and free of paragraph-location scaffolding — the strongest format in this shard.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is universal across all questions and activities — every stem or instruction injects a paragraph reference that teaches document navigation instead of operational substance.
True/false question explanations are consistently placeholders ('That requirement appears in 10-6-Xy') with zero instructional value.
Activity para_context fields embed paragraph references in the auxiliary context provided to learners, undermining the field's purpose of teaching the applicable rule.
Flashcards are clean and well-constructed across all four paragraphs — no scaffolding, appropriate retrieval targets, and procedurally relevant content.

One question (b1737128) has a meta-referential correct answer that cites 'the paragraph's criteria' instead of stating the actual operational criteria, making it both a scaffolding and an educational coherence defect.
All multiple-choice items have plausible, operationally grounded distractors that reflect realistic mistakes — distractor quality is a strength of this shard.

Every question stem opens with 'Under 10-7-X' and most explanations cite a paragraph sub-reference, substituting document trivia for operational substance — a systematic flaw across all 12 questions and all 5 activity para_context fields.

Four of five paragraphs have a true/false question whose stem verbatim restates the source rule as a true statement, directly duplicating the MC question for the same paragraph. The fifth T/F (10-7-2) covers a distinct concept but in equally thin format.
The 10-7-1 en route facilities question (b6f446f2) has a correct answer noticeably longer than its distractors, creating a surface cue.
The 10-7-1 flashcard (72139516) uses vague language — 'the appropriate stations or facilities' — that fails to identify the specific relay channels the source requires.
Activity para_context fields each begin with 'Under 10-7-X,' — the same paragraph-location scaffolding as the questions.
generation_guidance: Never prefix question stems or activity prompts with 'Under paragraph X,' 'Under the note to X,' or similar location references; stems must be self-contained operational scenarios or direct queries.

Explanations must teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception, not merely restate the correct answer or cite a paragraph number. When the source states only a duty, threshold, or sequence, explain that requirement directly without adding unsupported rationale about purpose or safety impact.
True/false explanations in particular must teach why the statement is correct, not merely confirm that the statement appears in the source material.

Match activity type to the tested skill: readback_check requires an ATC clearance (or instruction) that a pilot would actually read back. Pilot distress declarations, Autoland system transmissions, or other non-ATC-issued communications should use knowledge_check or a recognition format.

Flashcard backs must target one retrieval concept. When a front asks about a specific rule (e.g., handling when Mayday/Pan-Pan is not used), limit the back to that rule rather than appending a second distinct rule from a different sub-paragraph.

Cross-format reinforcement is appropriate when the same concept is tested through different retrieval modes (question, activity, flashcard), but avoid duplicating the same concept in the same format with near-identical wording.
Strip all paragraph-number prefixes from stems. Replace 'Under 10-1-X' with self-contained operational context or simply remove the prefix.

Do not generate true/false items by quoting the source text and asking whether it matches the book. True/false should test an operational distinction (e.g., 'A controller may transfer emergency responsibility whenever the aircraft enters another sector' yields False).

Write explanations that teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception — not a restatement of the answer. If the source gives a reason (e.g., fighter pilot workload justifies minimizing transmissions), include it. Do not invent reasons the source does not provide.
Activity para_context fields must state the operational rule, not 'Under paragraph 10-1-X.' The field exists to give the learner the relevant rule while working a scenario.
Ensure each paragraph's note blocks receive proportional item coverage. 10-1-6's note about hindrances from nearby aircraft (congestion, distraction, wake turbulence) currently has zero items.

For single-sentence paragraphs like 10-1-5, limit total item count and vary retrieval mode — three MC questions on the same sentence is excessive; one well-written question plus the existing activity and flashcard suffices.
Flashcard front must match the retrieval target. 'Who handles...' expects a person or role, not a procedure description. Frame the front to match the answer format.
Never embed paragraph numbers in question stems, activity contexts, or explanations. Operational items must be self-contained — the student is tested on ATC procedure, not document structure.

When writing explanations, teach the controlling rule and its operational scope. Do not settle for 'Per 10-2-X, [restatement of answer].' Include conditions, exceptions, and downstream actions where the source provides them.

True/false questions with verbatim-rule stems are borderline flat-recognition tasks. Prefer stems that set up an operational scenario or test a condition, leaving the rule as the answer rather than the question.

For directive_check activities, explanations should teach what the directive means operationally, not merely confirm its paragraph location. When the source provides a companion note (e.g., portable receivers may be loaned), include it to give the directive practical context.

The clean flashcards in this shard demonstrate good form: front identifies the retrieval target, back is one concise answer, and rote recall is reserved for exact minima, time windows, and prohibitions where precision matters.
Strip all paragraph-number references from stems, explanations, and para_context fields. The learner needs the operational rule, not its location in the order.
Write T/F stems as complete propositions ('True or False: [statement to evaluate]') rather than interrogatives disguised as T/F.
Keep explanations centered on the operational rule: what the controller must do, under what conditions, and what follows. Avoid restating the answer verbatim.
The spot_the_error and readback_check activity types are effective for phraseology-heavy paragraphs; replicate this pattern for other chapters with prescribed transmission wording.

For situation_action activities, ensure 'para_context' provides the controlling source text without paragraph-number scaffolding, so the learner can reason from the rule rather than recognize a citation.
Do not add qualifiers like 'solely' to source directives. If the source says 'Do not withhold landing clearance,' reproduce that instruction without softening or hedging.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or answer choices. Frame every item as an operational question — 'When recommending an emergency airport...' not 'Under 10-2-15...'.
True/false explanations must teach the controlling rule, not merely cite where in the order the rule appears.

Match distractor length to correct-answer length. When the correct answer is a prescribed advisory (e.g., the EOVM pilot advisory), break the pattern by giving distractors comparable operational detail rather than terse dismissals.
Flashcard backs must use the exact source factor names from the order, particularly for enumerated lists like the seven emergency airport recommendation factors.

Avoid questions that test which paragraph contains a cross-reference (b0f46446). Test the operational requirement itself: automated sources are a supplement, not a replacement, for controller judgment.

The situation-action format works well for these paragraphs — continue pairing peer-pressure scenarios with multi-factor decision-making. Ensure explanations connect the answer to the principle rather than citing a paragraph.

Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or activity prompts. Frame every item as an operational question — 'After receiving a report of illness on board, what information must the controller obtain?' not 'Under 10-2-19...'.

Remove systematic 'Only...' prefix patterns from distractor sets. When the correct answer lacks 'Only' but every distractor begins with it, the pattern reveals the answer regardless of content knowledge.
True/false explanations must teach the controlling rule, not merely cite where the rule appears in the order. Each T/F item needs a substantive explanation of the operational requirement.

Do not create a T/F question that merely restates a concept already tested by an MCQ in the same retrieval mode. If the MCQ asks 'Which of the following is a recognized orientation method?' the T/F 'The recognized methods are X, Y, Z' adds no new retrieval demand.

Flashcard backs must contain the teachable content, not a pointer to where the content can be found. 'Obtain the listed information' teaches nothing — list the specific information items or summarize them concretely.
When converting a T/F item to MCQ, ensure the distractors are plausible operational alternatives that a controller might actually consider, not obviously wrong answers.

The situation-action format works well for these procedural paragraphs — continue pairing peer-pressure scenarios with the requirement to follow prescribed procedures. Ensure explanations connect the answer to the controlling rule rather than citing a paragraph.

Strip all paragraph-location scaffolding from stems, explanations, and activity prompts. Frame every item as an operational question — 'What transponder code does ATC assign when a pilot verbally notifies of a hijacking?' not 'Under 10-2-6, what code is assigned...'

True/false explanations must teach the controlling rule, threshold, or condition, not merely confirm correctness or cite where in the order the answer appears. Each T/F needs a substantive explanation that states the operational requirement.

Verify sub-paragraph labels and source-structure references against the actual source blocks before finalizing items. The generation process hallucinated '10-2-7c' and a 'note under 10-2-7' that do not exist.

Maintain the flashcard quality standards observed in this shard: self-contained fronts that identify the concept being recalled, backs concise enough for one retrieval target, no paragraph references.

Situation-action para_context should frame the operational rule without citing paragraph numbers. The scenario itself (peer controller giving wrong advice) is an effective pattern that should be continued.

When the correct answer in a situation-action item embeds rule justification while distractors only state wrong actions, ensure the length gap does not become an answer cue. This may mean expanding distractor justifications or accepting that scenario-based items naturally produce slightly longer correct answers.
For phraseology items, include the facility-name placeholder or a representative facility name to match the complete prescribed phraseology template.

Strip all paragraph-location scaffolding from stems, explanations, and activity prompts. Frame every item as an operational question: 'When must a controller initiate ALNOT procedures for an aircraft experiencing abnormal simultaneous loss of radar and communications?' not 'Under 10-3-1, what action is required when...'

True/false explanations must teach the controlling rule, threshold, or condition — not merely confirm correctness or cite where in the order the answer appears. A substantive T/F explanation states when the rule applies, what triggers it, and what the controller must do.

Verify sub-paragraph labels against the actual source blocks. The generation process attributed content to '10-2-8b2' when the source organizes it under 10-2-8c2(b). Source-structure references that are wrong are worse than no references at all.

Balance choice lengths: when the correct answer embeds multiple conditions or rule elements, either tighten its wording or add comparable substance to distractors. The correct choice in activity 578ee2aa is over twice the length of the shortest distractor.

Maintain the flashcard quality standards observed in this shard: self-contained fronts that identify the concept being recalled, backs concise enough for one retrieval target, no paragraph references, and appropriate retrieval format.

Situation-action para_context should present the operational rule without citing paragraph numbers. The scenario pattern (peer controller giving wrong advice that the student must recognize and counter) is effective and should be continued.

For the 10-2-9 radar assistance techniques list, continue producing items that test individual techniques (rather than dumping all a-f into one question), but ensure cross-format coverage so each major technique appears in at least two formats.
Do not restate paragraph-location in reconstructed source_basis. The source_basis should be the operative rule text, not 'the instruction in paragraph 10-2-8.'
Write all stems, activity contexts, and explanations without paragraph-number references. Operational scenarios should stand on their own.
Match distractor length and specificity to the correct answer. Avoid leading phrases like 'Only' that signal a distractor.
Replace thin true/false items with multiple-choice questions that require applying the rule to a concrete operational situation rather than recognizing a restated source sentence.

For information-forwarding lists (10-3-2 and 10-3-3), generate 2-3 flashcards per paragraph that each cover a focused subset (e.g., identification details vs. status and search details). The full list is too long for a single retrieval target.
Use concrete, specific backs on flashcards: list the actual information items rather than grouping them with vague labels like 'last communication and position details.'
Avoid testing the same note or rule in two different paragraphs when the source text is identical. If a note is duplicated in the source, test it once, in the paragraph where it first appears.

For situation-action activities, keep the conflict-driven format (another controller proposes an incorrect shortcut) but tighten the correct-answer wording and remove paragraph references from the context.

When testing ALNOT rules, emphasize the operational trigger (ALNOT must precede RCC SAR action) and the specific distribution parameters (all centers, Area B circuits, 50 miles either side of route) rather than treating them as isolated trivia.

For responsibility transfer triggers (10-3-5), ensure questions distinguish between the three independent triggers rather than blurring them into a single condition. The 'any one' vs. 'all' distinction is the key operational understanding.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems. Operational ATC questions should ask about the procedure, condition, or decision without referencing 'Under 10-X-Y.'

Never embed paragraph numbers in explanations. Cite the operational rule, not its location: 'When an aircraft is overdue, affected IFR traffic must be restricted or suspended unless radar separation is used' — not 'Per 10-4-1.'
True_false explanations must teach the operational principle, not state where to find it. A thin 'That instruction appears in 10-X-Yb' explanation wastes the learning opportunity.
When a correct answer covers a compound procedure, keep it as concise as possible while preserving accuracy. Long correct answers next to short distractors create answer-length cues.

Flashcards produced in this shard are well-scoped and self-contained. Continue the pattern: each card targets one retrieval concept, states conditions clearly on the front, and gives a concise operational answer on the back.

Negative stems are defensible when the operational rule itself contains an exception clause (e.g., 'unless radar separation is used'), but prefer phrasing the stem to test the complete rule with the exception in the answer.

Replace every 'Under 10-X-Y' and 'Per 10-X-Y' prefix with operational context that identifies the situation by its controlling conditions, not by its document location. Example: instead of 'Under 10-4-3, how long must concurrence be maintained?' write 'After a 30-minute traffic suspension period for an overdue aircraft expires and normal ATC resumes, how long must the concurrence of other operators or pilots be maintained?'

For compound procedural answers that are naturally longer than distractors, balance the distractor text length or phrasing to avoid revealing the answer by length. Distractors should be equally substantive.

When a paragraph contains only a single sentence, limit coverage to one question or one activity rather than producing an MCQ, a T/F, and an activity that all test the same fact in slightly different wording.

Strengthen explanations by teaching the operational condition, exception, or required action instead of restating the answer. For example, an explanation for the 5-minute suspicious-activity rule should include that the report goes to OS/CIC per FAA Order JO 7610.4, not merely that 'the paragraph says to treat it as suspicious.'
Continue the flashcard generation pattern used in this shard — the cards identify a single procedural concept on the front and deliver a concise, accurate back without document scaffolding.

Do not prefix question stems, explanations, or activity context fields with paragraph citations such as 'Under 10-6-X' or 'Per 10-6-X'. The learner needs to apply the rule, not navigate the source document.

True/false explanations must teach the controlling rule, condition, or threshold — not merely confirm that the statement appears in a cited paragraph. State why the statement is true or false in operational terms.
Activity para_context should present the relevant rule in plain operational language without embedding paragraph identifiers, so the learner can apply the rule directly to the situation.

When the correct answer for a multiple-choice question is a list or multi-part criterion, ensure the correct answer states the actual operational conditions (e.g., 'where the aircraft was flying at last contact, where it was about to enter if near the boundary, or where its destination is located for non-communicating aircraft') rather than pointing to 'the paragraph's criteria.'

Maintain the shard's strength in distractor construction — plausible operational mistakes grounded in adjacent phase thresholds, common misconceptions about responsibility, or misapplications of related rules.

Flashcards for this shard were well-built from the start: single retrieval targets, procedurally relevant, no scaffolding. Use the same construction pattern for flashcards in other shards — a clear front identifying the concept, a concise back stating the procedure in one to three tightly coupled steps.

Never preface question stems or activity para_context with paragraph references. Use operational context instead: 'In a terminal environment…', 'When rerouting IFR aircraft…', 'For VFR aircraft near an emergency…'.

Avoid creating true/false questions whose stem is a verbatim restatement of a source rule set to True. Such items add no retrieval depth and duplicate existing MC coverage. If a T/F format is used, craft a stem that tests a genuine misconception or conditional distinction.
Flashcard backs must name the specific facilities, minima, or actions the source prescribes — not generic placeholders like 'appropriate stations.'
Match distractor length to correct answer length so the answer is not revealed by text volume alone.
Explanations should state the controlling rule in operational terms without citing paragraph numbers. The last sentence of the source block is the natural basis for the explanation.
shard_overviews: This shard covers foundational emergency determination and assistance rules from 10-1-1 through 10-1-3. All items are factually accurate against their source blocks. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: every question stem opens with 'Under 10-1-X' and many explanations reference paragraph numbers rather than teaching the controlling rule. Three true/false questions have explanations that merely cite the paragraph without teaching substance. One activity applies a readback_check format to a pilot distress declaration that is not a readback scenario. One flashcard back overloads two distinct rules from different sub-paragraphs. The distractors across all items are generally plausible operational misconceptions, and the activity situations provide useful operational context.

This shard covers four paragraphs (10-1-4 through 10-1-7) with 22 target items across three formats. All 13 question stems embed paragraph-location scaffolding, and four true/false items test source-quote recognition rather than operational understanding. Explanations are uniformly thin, restating answers or citing paragraph numbers instead of teaching the controlling rule or condition. Activities and flashcards are substantially stronger: only two of five activities and one of four flashcards need intervention. Sixteen of 22 items receive replacement decisions, all at minor or major severity. No items require removal or splitting.

This shard covers three operational paragraphs (10-2-1, 10-2-10, 10-2-11) with factually accurate content across all 22 items. No source-fidelity errors were found. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 18 of 22 items embed paragraph references in stems, explanations, or situation contexts, making items dependent on document trivia rather than self-contained operational knowledge. Four items also have thin explanations that merely restate the answer or cite the paragraph number. The three flashcards and one activity are clean. No items require removal or splitting.

Shard 4 of 13 covers three emergency-assistance paragraphs (10-2-12 K-9 Teams, 10-2-13 MANPADS Alert, 10-2-14 Unauthorized Laser Illumination). All items are source-accurate: every answer is correct, distractors are plausible operational mistakes, and activity types are well-chosen for their content. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding — nearly every question stem opens with 'Under 10-2-XX' and many explanations cite paragraph numbers instead of teaching the operational rule. Two true/false questions use interrogative stems ('what should ATC do?') rather than evaluable propositions. One knowledge_check answer adds the qualifier 'solely' not present in the source. Explanations consistently restate answers without connecting them to the controlling principle or operational context. No source-fidelity errors were found in any answer.

This shard covers four paragraphs (10-2-15 through 10-2-18) dealing with emergency airport recommendations, guidance methods, EOVM/EAM use, and volcanic ash procedures. The substantive content across all formats is generally accurate and the situation-action activities are well-constructed with realistic operational scenarios. Every item requires at least a minor replacement, but no items need removal or splitting. The dominant defect — afflicting 22 of 24 items — is paragraph-location scaffolding: stems and explanations that cite paragraph numbers instead of teaching operational principles. One flashcard (ee76f1f4) has a major source-fidelity failure where the back invents content (rescue services, length/surface) not present in the source and omits actual source factors (aircraft type, pilot qualifications, vectoring/homing capability). Three items exhibit answer-length cues where the correct choice is substantially longer than distractors.

This shard covers three short paragraphs (10-2-19, 10-2-2, 10-2-3) dealing with public-health reporting, emergency frequency changes, and aircraft orientation methods. The substantive content across all formats is generally accurate and the situation-action activities are well-constructed with realistic operational scenarios. Every item requires at least a minor replacement, two T/F questions are removed for redundancy, and one flashcard requires a more specific back. The dominant defect — afflicting 14 of 19 items — is paragraph-location scaffolding: stems and explanations that cite paragraph numbers instead of teaching operational principles. A systematic distractor-wording cue appears in two 10-2-19 items where all three distractors use the 'Only...' prefix while the correct answer does not. Two T/F items from 10-2-2 and 10-2-3 duplicate MCQ content in the same retrieval mode and are removed. The 10-2-2 and 10-2-3 flashcards are well-formed and require no intervention.

This shard covers four emergency-assistance topics (altitude for reception, emergency situation triggers, hijacked aircraft procedures, VFR weather difficulty) with accurate substantive content throughout. The dominant defect — afflicting 20 of 24 items — is paragraph-location scaffolding: stems, explanations, and activity prompts that cite paragraph numbers instead of teaching operational principles. Two items contain incorrect source-structure references (non-existent sub-paragraph '10-2-7c' and a phantom 'note under 10-2-7'), indicating the generation process hallucinated paragraph labels. The four flashcards are the strongest format in the shard: all are self-contained, specific, single-retrieval-target, and free of defects. Cross-format reinforcement is moderate but shows a bright spot in the hijack verification phraseology, which appears across three distinct activity types. No items require removal or splitting; every intervention is a targeted replacement.

This shard covers three paragraphs: radar assistance to VFR aircraft in weather difficulty (10-2-8), radar assistance techniques (10-2-9), and overdue aircraft/ALNOT initiation (10-3-1). The substantive content is accurate throughout — no items contain source-fidelity errors that would produce unsafe operational decisions. The dominant defect is paragraph-location scaffolding: 15 of 20 items embed paragraph references ('Under 10-2-8', 'Per 10-3-1b', etc.) in stems, explanations, or activity para_context fields, teaching document navigation instead of operational decision-making. Three items combine scaffolding with thin explanations that merely confirm correctness or cite paragraph numbers without teaching the controlling principle. Two items exhibit answer-length cues where the correct choice is noticeably longer than distractors. The three flashcards are the strongest format: all are self-contained, specific, single-retrieval-target, and free of defects. One source-structure error appears — item ee162594 cites a non-existent sub-paragraph '10-2-8b2' when the source organizes the content as 10-2-8c2(b). No items require removal or splitting; every intervention is a replace. Activities 23b1ada9 and 4a7a6127 (both 10-2-9) are correctly constructed and are retained without intervention.

This shard covers the overdue-aircraft information-forwarding chain (Terminal to ARTCC, ARTCC to RCC), ALNOT distribution and amendment rules, and RCC responsibility transfer triggers. Most items test operationally relevant content but are degraded by pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding ('Under 10-3-X'), thin true/false questions that merely restate source text, answer-length cues in several multiple-choice questions, overloaded or vague flashcard backs, and weak distractors beginning with 'Only.' One near-duplicate true/false question tests a note that is identically worded in both 10-3-2 and 10-3-3. The two situation-action activities for 10-3-2 and 10-3-3 are the strongest items — they present realistic operational conflicts — but still carry paragraph scaffolding in their context and explanation fields.

The shard covers four paragraphs (10-3-6 through 10-4-2) with sound operational content across all three formats. No items are factually wrong or educationally harmful. Every item, however, embeds paragraph-location scaffolding in either its stem or explanation — often both — and three true_false questions carry explanations that only cite a paragraph number rather than teach the operational principle. One activity has an answer-length differential that could cue the correct choice. The four flashcards are clean and require no changes.

This shard covers five paragraphs (10-4-3, 10-4-4, 10-5-1, 10-5-2, 10-6-1) with 24 items across three formats. The content is factually accurate and sourced, and all activities present operationally sound scenarios with plausible distractors. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding — nearly every question stem, explanation, and activity context opens with 'Under 10-X-Y' or 'Per 10-X-Y', teaching document navigation instead of operational substance. Two questions suffer from answer-length cues where the correct choice is substantially longer than distractors. One T/F question from the single-sentence paragraph 10-6-1 is redundant with its companion MCQ. Explanations are generally thin restatements rather than operational teachings, though none fabricate unsupported rationales. All five flashcards are well-formed and require no intervention.

This shard covers the four oceanic emergency procedures paragraphs (10-6-2 through 10-6-5) with 12 questions, 6 activities, and 4 flashcards. The underlying content is substantively accurate and all items correctly identify the controlling rules. However, systemic paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect — every question stem opens with 'Under 10-6-X', most explanations cite paragraph numbers rather than teaching the rule, and activity para_context fields inject paragraph references into instructional material. Several true/false questions have explanations consisting solely of 'That requirement appears in 10-6-Xy' which teaches nothing. One question uses a meta-referential answer ('the paragraph's criteria') that fails to state the operational rule. The four flashcards are clean and well-constructed. All 18 non-flashcard items require replacement to strip paragraph scaffolding and upgrade explanations to teach controlling principles.

This shard covers five short, single-rule paragraphs (10-7-1 through 10-7-5) about ground missile emergency procedures. The source rules are correctly represented in the generated items, but systematic paragraph-location scaffolding and four educationally thin true/false duplicates degrade quality across all five paragraphs. One flashcard is context-light and one question has an answer-length cue. After remediation, the shard will have 8 MC questions (down from 12), 5 activities, and 5 flashcards.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-1-1' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation says 'Per 10-1-1c, handle it as though it were an emergency,' which restates the answer without teaching the controlling rule. The stem requires no paragraph-number knowledge to answer operational content. The automated flag 'negative_stem' is incorrect; the stem asks 'how is the situation handled,' which is a positive operational question.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-1c states: 'If the words Mayday or Pan-Pan are not used but you believe an emergency or an urgent situation exists, handle it as though it were an emergency.'

match.question_text: Under 10-1-1, if the words 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' are not used but you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists, how is the situation handled?

replacements.question_text: If a pilot never uses the words 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' but you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists, how should the situation be handled?
replacements.explanation: When a controller believes an emergency or urgent situation exists, the controller must handle the situation as an emergency even if the pilot has not used the formal distress word 'Mayday' or the urgency word 'Pan-Pan.' The controller's independent assessment that an emergency exists, not the pilot's choice of formal words, governs the emergency response.

choices.text: Handle it as though it were an emergency.
Wait for a formal declaration before using emergency procedures.
Treat it only as an urgency condition until the pilot says 'Mayday.'
Continue normal operations unless radar shows an immediate loss of separation.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-1-1' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation cites 'Per 10-1-1b' rather than teaching the prescribed phraseology rule. The question tests exact recall of prescribed phraseology, which is appropriate for this content, but the scaffolding and thin explanation degrade its educational value.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-1b states: 'A pilot who encounters a Distress condition should declare an emergency by beginning the initial communication with the word Mayday, preferably repeated three times.'

match.question_text: Under 10-1-1, what word should a pilot encountering a Distress condition use to begin the initial communication?

replacements.question_text: What word should a pilot encountering a Distress condition use to begin the initial communication?
replacements.explanation: A pilot encountering a Distress condition should begin the initial communication with the word 'Mayday,' preferably repeated three times. This prescribed word signals a Distress condition to ATC. For an Urgency condition, the word 'Pan-Pan' is used in the same manner. The two conditions use distinct prescribed words.

choices.text: Mayday, preferably repeated three times.
Pan-Pan, preferably repeated three times.
Emergency, stated once after the aircraft call sign.
Distress, repeated until ATC acknowledges the transmission.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-1-1' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation cites 'Per 10-1-1b' rather than teaching the prescribed phraseology rule. The question tests exact recall of prescribed phraseology, which is appropriate per the harness for defined terms, but the scaffolding degrades its self-contained quality.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-1b states: 'For an Urgency condition, the word Pan-Pan should be used in the same manner.' The 'same manner' refers to beginning the initial communication with the word, preferably repeated three times.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-1, what word should a pilot encountering an Urgency condition use in the initial communication?

replacements.question_text: What word should a pilot encountering an Urgency condition use in the initial communication?
replacements.explanation: For an Urgency condition, the prescribed word is 'Pan-Pan,' used preferably repeated three times at the start of the initial communication. This is distinct from a Distress condition, which uses 'Mayday' in the same manner. Recognizing which prescribed word corresponds to which condition is essential for correctly interpreting a pilot's declaration.

choices.text: Pan-Pan, used in the same manner as Mayday for distress.
Mayday, because urgency and distress use the same word.
Urgency, repeated three times.
Standby, followed by the nature of the problem.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-1-1' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation states only 'That instruction is stated in 10-1-1d,' which is a pure paragraph-reference with zero instructional substance. A true/false item needs an explanation that teaches why the statement is correct, not merely that it appears in the source.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-1d states: 'Because of the infinite variety of possible emergency situations, specific procedures cannot be prescribed. However, when you believe an emergency exists or is imminent, select and pursue a course of action which appears to be most appropriate under the circumstances and which most nearly conforms to the instructions in this manual.'

match.question_text: Under 10-1-1, because of the infinite variety of possible emergency situations, specific procedures cannot be prescribed and the controller should pursue the course of action that appears most appropriate under the circumstances.

replacements.question_text: Because of the infinite variety of possible emergency situations, specific procedures cannot be prescribed and the controller should pursue the course of action that appears most appropriate under the circumstances.
replacements.explanation: Emergency situations are too varied for a single prescribed procedure to cover every case. The controller must exercise judgment to select the course of action that appears most appropriate under the specific circumstances while conforming as nearly as possible to the instructions in the manual. This grants controllers discretion to adapt their response to the situation rather than following a rigid script.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The activity uses a readback_check format but presents a pilot distress declaration (Mayday call) as the 'clearance' field. Controllers do not read back pilot Mayday declarations; the readback_check format requires an ATC-issued clearance or instruction that a pilot would actually read back. The activity tests recognition of correct distress phraseology, which is better suited to a knowledge_check or recognition format. The existing distractors change the distress word, omit repetitions, and omit the word entirely, all of which are valid operational distinctions.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-1b states: 'A pilot who encounters a Distress condition should declare an emergency by beginning the initial communication with the word Mayday, preferably repeated three times. For an Urgency condition, the word Pan-Pan should be used in the same manner.'

content.clearance: MAYDAY, MAYDAY, MAYDAY, ENGINE FAILURE, REQUEST IMMEDIATE LANDING.

choices.text: Mayday, Mayday, Mayday, engine failure, request immediate landing.
Pan-Pan, Pan-Pan, Pan-Pan, engine failure, request immediate landing.
Mayday, engine failure, request immediate landing.
Engine failure, request immediate landing.

content.explanation: Per 10-1-1, a pilot encountering a Distress condition should begin the initial communication with the word 'Mayday,' preferably repeated three times.

content.question_text: Which transmission correctly follows the prescribed phraseology for a pilot declaring a Distress condition?

choices.text: Mayday, Mayday, Mayday, engine failure, request immediate landing.
Pan-Pan, Pan-Pan, Pan-Pan, engine failure, request immediate landing.
Mayday, engine failure, request immediate landing.
Engine failure, request immediate landing.

content.explanation: A pilot encountering a Distress condition should begin the initial communication with the word 'Mayday,' preferably repeated three times. 'Pan-Pan' is reserved for Urgency conditions, not Distress. Omitting the distress word entirely fails to declare an emergency using the prescribed phraseology.

operations.problem: The back combines two distinct rules from different sub-paragraphs: 'Handle it as though it were an emergency' (from 10-1-1c about Mayday/Pan-Pan not being used) and 'choose the course of action that best fits the circumstances' (from 10-1-1d about exercising judgment when no specific procedures exist). The front asks specifically about the situation where Mayday/Pan-Pan is not used, so the back should focus on the 10-1-1c rule. Appending the 10-1-1d rule dilutes the retrieval target.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-1c states: 'If the words Mayday or Pan-Pan are not used but you believe an emergency or an urgent situation exists, handle it as though it were an emergency.'

match.front: How is a situation handled if 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' is not used but you believe an emergency exists?
match.back: Handle it as though it were an emergency and choose the course of action that best fits the circumstances.

replacements.front: How is a situation handled if 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' is not used but you believe an emergency exists?
replacements.back: Handle it as though it were an emergency.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-1-2' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation cites 'Per 10-1-2a' and restates the answer without elaboration. The automated flag 'negative_stem' is incorrect; the stem asks 'what should the controller do,' which is a positive operational question. The distractors are plausible operational errors and the correct answer is accurate.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-2a states: 'Use the information provided or solicit more information as necessary to assist the distressed aircraft.'

match.question_text: Under 10-1-2, if the information initially provided is not enough to assist the distressed aircraft, what should the controller do?

replacements.question_text: If the information initially provided by a distressed aircraft is not enough for the controller to assist effectively, what should the controller do?
replacements.explanation: The controller must actively solicit additional information as necessary to assist the distressed aircraft. The controller does not passively wait for the pilot to volunteer more details. Using the information already provided, the controller determines what more is needed and asks for it to build an adequate picture of the situation.

choices.text: Solicit more information as necessary to assist the distressed aircraft.
Wait until the pilot volunteers more information without prompting.
Terminate the exchange and let the next controller gather the remaining information.
Ignore missing details as long as the aircraft has declared an emergency.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-1-2' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation cites 'Per 10-1-2a' and restates the answer without elaboration. The question tests an important operational concept (controller may recommend, but not override, the pilot's chosen course of action during an emergency) and the distractors are well-constructed, but the scaffolding and thin explanation weaken the item.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-2a states: 'Provide assistance that is consistent with the requests of the pilot. If you believe an alternative course of action may prove more beneficial, transmit your recommendation(s) to the pilot.'

match.question_text: Under 10-1-2, what should a controller do if an alternative course of action may prove more beneficial to the distressed aircraft?

replacements.question_text: If a controller believes an alternative course of action may prove more beneficial to a distressed aircraft than what the pilot requested, what should the controller do?
replacements.explanation: The controller must provide assistance consistent with the pilot's requests while also transmitting a recommendation if an alternative course of action may be more beneficial. The controller does not issue the alternative as a mandatory instruction; the pilot in command retains authority and responsibility for the operation of the aircraft. The recommendation is advisory, not a directive.

choices.text: Transmit the recommendation to the pilot.
Issue the alternative as a mandatory instruction regardless of the pilot's request.
Withhold the alternative because controllers may not recommend changes during an emergency.
Wait until the aircraft lands before advising that a better alternative existed.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-1-2' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation cites 'Per 10-1-2b' and lists the five information elements without teaching their operational significance. This question tests recall of a defined information set, which is appropriate, but the paragraph-location scaffolding in both stem and explanation needs removal.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-2b states that an Emergency Autoland system may transmit: callsign, that Emergency Autoland has been activated, position relative to a nearby airport, the intended emergency landing airport and planned landing runway, and an ETE to the emergency landing airport.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-2, which information may an Emergency Autoland system transmit?

replacements.question_text: Which information may an Emergency Autoland system transmit to ATC during an emergency?
replacements.explanation: An Emergency Autoland system may transmit five elements: the aircraft's call sign, notification that Emergency Autoland has been activated, the aircraft's position including mileage and direction relative to a nearby airport, the intended emergency landing airport and planned landing runway, and the estimated time en route to that airport. This full set of information enables the controller to respond effectively to the emergency.

choices.text: The call sign, Emergency Autoland activation, position, intended emergency landing airport and runway, and ETE.
Only the emergency airport identifier and the operator's phone number.
Only the call sign and a transponder code because landing information is sent separately.
Only the aircraft type and the nearest ARTCC sector frequency.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under the note to 10-1-2' (paragraph-location scaffolding that references a note rather than even the paragraph itself). The explanation states only 'That note is stated in 10-1-2,' which is a pure paragraph-location reference with zero instructional substance. A true/false item needs an explanation that teaches why the statement is correct.
operations.source_basis: Note 2 to paragraph 10-1-2 states: 'In the event of frequency congestion, an Emergency Autoland system may transmit on 121.5 or CTAF instead of the last assigned ATC frequency.'

match.question_text: Under the note to 10-1-2, during frequency congestion an Emergency Autoland system may transmit on 121.5 or CTAF instead of the last assigned ATC frequency.

replacements.question_text: During frequency congestion, an Emergency Autoland system may transmit on 121.5 or CTAF instead of the last assigned ATC frequency.
replacements.explanation: An Emergency Autoland system may transmit on 121.5 (the emergency frequency) or CTAF (Common Traffic Advisory Frequency) when frequency congestion makes the last assigned ATC frequency unusable. This alternate transmission path allows controllers to receive emergency Autoland declarations even when the primary assigned frequency is congested.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-1-3' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation states only 'Per 10-1-3, provide maximum assistance to aircraft in distress,' which restates the answer without elaboration. The explanation does not teach the full scope of assistance that 'maximum' entails or the resources available to the controller.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-3 states: 'Provide maximum assistance to aircraft in distress. Enlist the services of available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the Federal Communications Commission, as well as their emergency services and facilities, when the pilot requests or when you deem necessary.'

match.question_text: Under 10-1-3, what level of help is provided to aircraft in distress?

replacements.question_text: What level of assistance must be provided to aircraft in distress?
replacements.explanation: Controllers must provide maximum assistance to aircraft in distress. This includes enlisting available radar facilities operated by the FAA, military services, and the Federal Communications Commission, along with their emergency services and facilities, when the pilot requests them or when the controller deems them necessary.

choices.text: Maximum assistance.
Only the minimum assistance needed to keep the aircraft clear of other traffic.
Assistance limited to the services available within the controller's own facility.
Assistance only after approval from the operations supervisor or CIC.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-1-3' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation cites 'Per 10-1-3' and lists the resources without teaching the operational principle that the controller may use own judgment to enlist resources. The question tests recall of available resources, which is appropriate, but the scaffolding needs removal.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-3 states: 'Enlist the services of available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the Federal Communications Commission, as well as their emergency services and facilities, when the pilot requests or when you deem necessary.'

match.question_text: Under 10-1-3, what outside resources may be enlisted to assist an aircraft in distress?

replacements.question_text: What outside resources may a controller enlist to assist an aircraft in distress?
replacements.explanation: A controller may enlist available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the Federal Communications Commission, as well as those organizations' emergency services and facilities. These resources may be enlisted when the pilot requests them or when the controller independently deems them necessary. The controller is not limited to their own facility's resources.

choices.text: Available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the FCC, along with their emergency services and facilities.
Only FAA radar facilities because distress coordination may not use military or FCC resources.
Only airport rescue and firefighting units located on the destination airport.
Only the resources specifically requested by the pilot, never those the controller deems necessary.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-1-3' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation states only 'That standard is stated in 10-1-3,' which is a pure paragraph-location reference with zero instructional substance. A true/false item needs an explanation that teaches the underlying rule.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-3 states: 'Enlist the services of available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the Federal Communications Commission, as well as their emergency services and facilities, when the pilot requests or when you deem necessary.'

match.question_text: Under 10-1-3, the controller may enlist available radar and emergency-service resources when the pilot requests them or when the controller deems them necessary.

replacements.question_text: The controller may enlist available radar and emergency-service resources when the pilot requests them or when the controller deems them necessary.
replacements.explanation: The controller may enlist available radar facilities and emergency services from the FAA, military services, and FCC when the pilot requests assistance or when the controller independently determines the resources are necessary. The controller is not required to wait for a pilot request before enlisting outside resources.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The para_context field within the activity content opens with 'Under 10-1-3' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The activity itself is well-constructed with a rich operational situation, plausible distractors, and a substantive correct answer. Only the para_context scaffolding needs correction; the situation and choices remain unchanged.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 10-1-3 states: 'Provide maximum assistance to aircraft in distress. Enlist the services of available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the Federal Communications Commission, as well as their emergency services and facilities, when the pilot requests or when you deem necessary.'

content.situation: An aircraft in distress is losing communications and radar coverage near the edge of your area. Military radar and other emergency services are available, but another controller says you should wait unless the pilot explicitly asks for outside help.
content.para_context: Under 10-1-3, provide maximum assistance to aircraft in distress. Enlist the services of available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the Federal Communications Commission, as well as their emergency services and facilities, when the pilot requests or when you deem necessary.

choices.text: Provide maximum assistance and enlist available radar and emergency services when the pilot requests it or when you deem it necessary.
Wait for the pilot to request each outside service individually because ATC may not initiate outside assistance on its own judgment.
Use only FAA facilities because military and FCC resources are outside the scope of distress assistance.
Reduce involvement once the aircraft nears another facility because maximum assistance applies only inside your own airspace.

content.explanation: Per 10-1-3, provide maximum assistance and use available FAA, military, FCC, and emergency-service resources when requested or when you deem necessary.

content.situation: An aircraft in distress is losing communications and radar coverage near the edge of your area. Military radar and other emergency services are available, but another controller says you should wait unless the pilot explicitly asks for outside help.
content.para_context: Provide maximum assistance to aircraft in distress. Enlist the services of available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the Federal Communications Commission, as well as their emergency services and facilities, when the pilot requests or when you deem necessary.

choices.text: Provide maximum assistance and enlist available radar and emergency services when the pilot requests it or when you deem it necessary.
Wait for the pilot to request each outside service individually because ATC may not initiate outside assistance on its own judgment.
Use only FAA facilities because military and FCC resources are outside the scope of distress assistance.
Reduce involvement once the aircraft nears another facility because maximum assistance applies only inside your own airspace.

content.explanation: Provide maximum assistance and enlist available FAA, military, FCC, and emergency-service resources when the pilot requests them or when you deem necessary. The controller may initiate enlistment of outside resources based on independent judgment and is not limited to waiting for a pilot request or to resources within a single facility.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 10-1-4' paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation restates the correct answer without teaching the transfer condition that distinguishes the rule from a simple delegation of authority.
operations.source_basis: If you are in communication with an aircraft in distress, handle the emergency and coordinate and direct the activities of assisting facilities. Transfer this responsibility to another facility only when you feel better handling of the emergency will result.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-4, if you are in communication with an aircraft in distress, what are you expected to do?

replacements.question_text: If you are in communication with an aircraft in distress, what are you expected to do?
replacements.explanation: The controller in communication with an aircraft in distress handles the emergency and coordinates and directs the activities of assisting facilities. This responsibility transfers to another facility only when the transferring controller determines that better handling of the emergency will result.

choices.text: Handle the emergency and coordinate and direct the activities of assisting facilities.
Maintain communications only and leave all other coordination to the center automatically.
Transfer responsibility immediately to the next facility once the aircraft approaches its boundary.
Handle the emergency only until rescue agencies respond, then cease all ATC coordination.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 10-1-4' paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation is thin and does not clarify that the transfer standard is the controller's judgment, not an external trigger.
operations.source_basis: Transfer this responsibility to another facility only when you feel better handling of the emergency will result.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-4, when may emergency-handling responsibility be transferred to another facility?

replacements.question_text: When may emergency-handling responsibility for an aircraft in distress be transferred to another facility?
replacements.explanation: Transfer of emergency-handling responsibility is permitted only when the transferring controller determines that better handling of the emergency will result. The transfer is not automatic upon an airspace boundary crossing, another facility's offer, or a pilot request.

choices.text: Only when the transferring controller determines better handling of the emergency will result.
Whenever the aircraft enters another controller's airspace.
As soon as another facility offers to take the aircraft, regardless of circumstances.
Only after the pilot explicitly requests a transfer.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 10-1-4' paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation restates the answer without teaching the operational reason centers are the destination for emergency data.
operations.source_basis: When you receive information about an aircraft in distress, forward detailed data to the center in whose area the emergency exists. Centers serve as the central points for collecting information and for coordinating with SAR.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-4, when you receive information about an aircraft in distress, what must be forwarded and to whom?

replacements.question_text: When you receive information about an aircraft in distress, what must be forwarded and to whom?
replacements.explanation: Upon receiving information about an aircraft in distress, a controller must forward detailed data to the ARTCC in whose area the emergency exists. ARTCCs serve as the central collection point for emergency information and coordinate with search and rescue.

choices.text: Forward detailed data to the center in whose area the emergency exists.
Forward a brief summary only to the destination airport tower.
Forward the information only if the aircraft is on an IFR flight plan.
Wait until the aircraft lands before forwarding the emergency information.

operations.problem: True/false stem quotes the source text verbatim with 'Under the note to 10-1-4' scaffolding, testing source-quote recognition instead of operational understanding. Explanation states only the paragraph location without teaching the rule's significance.
operations.source_basis: Notifying the center about a VFR aircraft emergency allows provision of IFR separation if considered necessary.

match.question_text: Under the note to 10-1-4, notifying the center about a VFR aircraft emergency allows provision of IFR separation if considered necessary.

replacements.question_text: What operational benefit does notifying the ARTCC about a VFR aircraft emergency provide?
replacements.explanation: Notifying the ARTCC about a VFR aircraft emergency allows the provision of IFR separation if considered necessary. This is a specific operational benefit of center notification for VFR emergencies, distinct from the general emergency-data forwarding requirement.

choices.text: It allows provision of IFR separation if considered necessary.
It automatically upgrades the VFR flight to an IFR flight plan.
It relieves the controller of further coordination responsibility for that aircraft.
It suspends all VFR traffic in the area until the emergency is resolved.

operations.problem: Card is typed as 'definition' but contains a procedural responsibility. Front asks 'Who handles...' expecting a person or role, but the back answers with a procedure description ('You handle and coordinate...'). The question-answer alignment is disjoint.
operations.source_basis: If you are in communication with an aircraft in distress, handle the emergency and coordinate and direct the activities of assisting facilities. Transfer this responsibility to another facility only when you feel better handling of the emergency will result.

match.front: Who handles an aircraft emergency when you are in communication with the aircraft in distress?
match.back: You handle and coordinate the emergency, transferring that responsibility only when better handling will result.

replacements.front: What is your responsibility when you are in communication with an aircraft in distress?
replacements.back: Handle the emergency and coordinate and direct the activities of assisting facilities. Transfer responsibility to another facility only when you feel better handling of the emergency will result.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 10-1-5' paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation restates the answer verbatim instead of teaching the scope of the coordination requirement.
operations.source_basis: Coordinate efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-5, for which aircraft should efforts be coordinated?

replacements.question_text: For which aircraft situations must controllers coordinate assistance efforts?
replacements.explanation: Controllers must coordinate efforts to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status. The coordination requirement applies regardless of whether the aircraft is operating under IFR or VFR.

choices.text: Any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.
Only aircraft that have already declared an emergency.
Only overdue IFR aircraft, not lost or emergency-status VFR aircraft.
Only aircraft for which a center has already issued an ALNOT.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 10-1-5' paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation says 'The paragraph says to coordinate efforts to the extent possible,' which is a paragraph-location explanation rather than an operational teaching point.
operations.source_basis: Coordinate efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-5, to what extent are efforts coordinated to assist an aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status?

replacements.question_text: To what extent must coordination efforts be made for an aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status?
replacements.explanation: Coordination efforts must be made to the extent possible. The standard is not conditional on confirmed details, sector boundaries, or formal search and rescue assumption of the case.

choices.text: To the extent possible.
Only after every relevant detail has been confirmed.
Only within the controller's own sector unless the pilot asks for wider coordination.
Only after search and rescue has formally assumed the case.

operations.source_basis: Coordinate efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-5, efforts are coordinated to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

replacements.question_text: A controller learns a VFR aircraft is 45 minutes overdue at its destination with no radio contact. According to coordination requirements, what should the controller do?
replacements.explanation: Controllers must coordinate efforts to the extent possible for any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status. The requirement applies regardless of flight rules, so the controller should begin coordination without waiting for a confirmed emergency declaration or an ALNOT.

choices.text: Coordinate efforts to the extent possible because the aircraft is believed overdue.
Wait 15 more minutes because the aircraft is VFR and a longer overdue threshold applies.
Coordinate only if the pilot had filed an IFR flight plan.
Take no action unless the destination airport reports the aircraft missing.

operations.source_basis: Coordinate efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

content.para_context: Under 10-1-5, coordinate efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

choices.text: Coordinate assistance efforts to the extent possible because the section applies to aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.
Wait for positive confirmation of an emergency before coordinating because a belief that the aircraft is overdue is not enough.
Coordinate only if the aircraft is IFR because overdue VFR flights are outside the section.
