content.explanation: Per 10-1-5, coordination should begin to the extent possible for any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

content.situation: A flight is believed overdue and contact has not been reestablished. Another controller wants to wait for confirmation that the aircraft is actually in distress before coordinating with other facilities or services.
content.para_context: Coordinate efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

choices.text: Coordinate assistance efforts to the extent possible for the overdue aircraft.
Wait for positive confirmation of an emergency before coordinating because a belief that the aircraft is overdue is not enough.
Coordinate only if the aircraft is IFR because overdue VFR flights are outside the coordination requirement.
Limit coordination to the current sector because wider coordination begins only after a center declares an ALNOT.

content.explanation: Aircraft believed overdue fall within the coordination requirement. The controller should begin coordinating assistance efforts to the extent possible without waiting for confirmation of an actual emergency or for a center to issue an ALNOT.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 10-1-6' paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation restates the answer without teaching the extension of the rule to off-airport emergencies involving equipment movement.
operations.source_basis: When an emergency occurs on the airport proper, control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled. This also applies when routes within the airport proper are required for movement of local emergency equipment going to or from an emergency which occurs outside the airport proper.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-6, when an emergency occurs on the airport proper, what must be done with other air and ground traffic?

replacements.question_text: When an emergency occurs on the airport proper, what must be done with other air and ground traffic?
replacements.explanation: During an airport ground emergency, controllers must control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled. This requirement also applies when routes within the airport proper are needed for movement of local emergency equipment going to or from an emergency outside the airport proper.

choices.text: Control it to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled.
Allow it to continue normally unless the pilot of the emergency aircraft requests otherwise.
Stop only departing aircraft because ground traffic is excluded from this paragraph.
Protect only the active runway and not any adjacent taxiway or surface movement areas.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 10-1-6' paragraph-location scaffolding and references 'this traffic-control requirement' without establishing the requirement first, making it dependent on prior question context rather than self-contained.

replacements.question_text: The requirement to control air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in an emergency area also applies when the emergency is outside the airport proper. Under what condition does this extension apply?
replacements.explanation: The traffic-control requirement extends to emergencies outside the airport proper when routes within the airport proper are needed for movement of local emergency equipment going to or from that external emergency.

choices.text: When routes within the airport proper are needed for local emergency equipment going to or from an outside emergency.
Only when the emergency equipment has already reached the off-airport scene and is returning to the field.
Only when the emergency involves an aircraft accident and not any other airport emergency.
Only when the airport manager specifically requests movement-area protection.

operations.problem: True/false stem quotes the paragraph text verbatim with 'Under 10-1-6' scaffolding, testing source-quote recognition. Explanation states only 'That requirement is stated in 10-1-6b,' providing no operational teaching.
operations.source_basis: Workload permitting, monitor the progress of emergency vehicles responding to a situation. If necessary, provide available information to assist responders in finding the accident/incident scene.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-6, workload permitting, controllers monitor the progress of emergency vehicles and, if necessary, provide available information to help responders find the accident or incident scene.

replacements.question_text: After an emergency occurs on or near the airport, what responsibility does a controller have regarding emergency vehicles, workload permitting?
replacements.explanation: Workload permitting, controllers should monitor the progress of emergency vehicles responding to a situation. If necessary, the controller provides available information to assist responders in locating the accident or incident scene.

choices.text: Monitor the progress of emergency vehicles and provide available information to help responders find the scene if necessary.
Assume full direction of emergency vehicle movements on the airport surface.
Clear all runways so emergency vehicles can use them without restriction.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 10-1-7' paragraph-location scaffolding. operations.source_basis: Pilots of military fighter-type aircraft, normally single engine, experiencing or anticipating loss of engine power or control may execute a flameout pattern in an emergency situation. Circumstances may dictate that the pilot, depending on the position and nature of the emergency, modify the pattern based on actual emergency recovery requirements.

replacements.question_text: What emergency recovery pattern may a pilot of a military fighter-type aircraft execute when experiencing or anticipating loss of engine power or control?
replacements.explanation: Pilots of military fighter-type aircraft, normally single engine, may execute a flameout pattern when experiencing or anticipating loss of engine power or control. The pilot may modify the pattern based on the position and nature of the emergency and actual recovery requirements.

choices.text: A flameout pattern.
A standard holding pattern.
An emergency descent procedure.
A precautionary approach.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 10-1-7' paragraph-location scaffolding. Correct answer mentions only frequency and transponder code changes but omits 'radio transmissions held to a minimum' which the source states as part of the same operational principle.
operations.source_basis: Radio frequency and transponder code changes should be avoided and radio transmissions held to a minimum, especially when the aircraft experiencing the emergency is at low altitude. The pilot's full attention is required to maintain control of the aircraft.

match.question_text: Under 10-1-7, what should generally be avoided during an inflight emergency involving a military fighter-type aircraft?

replacements.question_text: During an inflight emergency involving a military fighter-type aircraft, what communication practices should controllers follow?
replacements.explanation: The high workload on military fighter-type aircraft pilots during inflight emergencies means radio frequency and transponder code changes should be avoided and radio transmissions held to a minimum, especially at low altitude where the pilot's full attention is required for aircraft control.

choices.text: Avoid frequency and transponder code changes and minimize radio transmissions.
Continue normal communications because fighter pilots are trained for high-workload environments.
Increase radio transmissions to ensure the pilot maintains situational awareness.
Switch the aircraft to the emergency frequency immediately upon first report of trouble.

operations.problem: True/false stem quotes the paragraph text verbatim with 'Under 10-1-7' scaffolding, testing source-quote recognition. Explanation states only 'That authority is stated in 10-1-7c,' teaching nothing about the operational significance of practice emergency approaches.

operations.problem: The para_context field opens with 'Under 10-1-7' paragraph-location scaffolding, exposing the paragraph number to the learner during a scenario exercise.
operations.source_basis: The design and complexity of military fighter-type aircraft places an extremely high workload on the pilot during an inflight emergency. Radio frequency and transponder code changes should be avoided and radio transmissions held to a minimum, especially when the aircraft experiencing the emergency is at low altitude. Pilots may execute a flameout pattern in an emergency situation, and circumstances may dictate that the pilot modify the pattern based on actual emergency recovery requirements.

content.situation: A military fighter-type aircraft at low altitude reports a possible engine failure. Another controller wants to issue a new transponder code, a frequency change, and several follow-up questions before the aircraft reaches the field.
content.para_context: Under 10-1-7, military fighter-type aircraft place an extremely high workload on the pilot during an inflight emergency. Radio frequency and transponder code changes should be avoided and radio transmissions held to a minimum, especially at low altitude. Pilots may execute a flameout pattern in an emergency, and the pattern may be modified based on actual recovery requirements.

choices.text: Avoid frequency and transponder changes and keep transmissions to a minimum while accommodating the fighter emergency recovery as needed.
Issue the frequency and code changes immediately because standardized ATC housekeeping items take priority during fighter emergencies.
Require the pilot to fly a fixed flameout pattern exactly as published regardless of the actual emergency.
Use normal-length transmissions because minimizing radio traffic applies only after the aircraft climbs to a safe altitude.

content.explanation: Per 10-1-7, avoid frequency and code changes and minimize transmissions during fighter emergencies, especially at low altitude.

content.situation: A military fighter-type aircraft at low altitude reports a possible engine failure. Another controller wants to issue a new transponder code, a frequency change, and several follow-up questions before the aircraft reaches the field.

Radio frequency and transponder code changes should be avoided and radio transmissions held to a minimum, especially at low altitude. choices.text: Avoid frequency and transponder changes and keep transmissions to a minimum while accommodating the fighter emergency recovery as needed.
Issue the frequency and code changes immediately because standardized ATC housekeeping items take priority during fighter emergencies.

content.explanation: During a fighter emergency, especially at low altitude, the pilot's full attention is required to maintain aircraft control. Frequency changes, transponder code changes, and unnecessary radio transmissions must be avoided to minimize pilot workload.

replacements.explanation: The minimum required information for inflight emergencies is aircraft identification and type, the nature of the emergency, and the pilot's desires. Assistance begins as soon as enough information has been obtained upon which to act; additional information can be collected after initiating action as necessary.

choices.text: Aircraft identification and type, the nature of the emergency, and the pilot's desires.
Aircraft altitude, fuel remaining, and the number of people on board.
The departure airport, destination airport, and aircraft color.

However, request the position of an aircraft that is not visually sighted or displayed on radar if the location is not given by the pilot.'

replacements.explanation: Normally, do not request the supplemental information from military fighter-type aircraft operating at low altitudes (for example, on approach, immediately after departure, or on a low-level route). However, request the position of an aircraft that is not visually sighted or displayed on radar if the location is not given by the pilot.

choices.text: Normally do not request it, except ask for position if the aircraft is not sighted or on radar and the location was not given.
Request the full supplemental list immediately because low-altitude fighter pilots are expected to provide detailed emergency updates.
Never ask the pilot for position, even if the aircraft is not sighted and no location was given.
Treat the supplemental list as optional only for multiengine military aircraft and not fighter-type aircraft.

content.situation: A low-altitude military fighter reports an emergency and gives aircraft identification, the nature of the problem, and what the pilot wants. Another controller wants to delay assistance until every supplemental information item has been collected first.
content.para_context: Under 10-2-1, start assistance as soon as enough information has been obtained upon which to act. Minimum required information for inflight emergencies is the aircraft identification and type, the nature of the emergency, and the pilot's desires. After initiating action, obtain other pertinent items as necessary. Normally, do not request the additional information from low-altitude military fighter-type aircraft, though request the position if the aircraft is not visually sighted or displayed on radar and the location is not given.

choices.text: Start assistance once you have enough information to act, then obtain additional pertinent items as necessary without overloading the low-altitude fighter pilot.
Delay all assistance until every listed supplemental item has been collected from the pilot.
Treat the flight as nonemergency until fuel, weather, souls on board, and navigation equipment have all been reported.
Avoid asking for the fighter's position even if it is not on radar and the pilot has not provided it.

content.explanation: Per 10-2-1, act once enough information is available, then collect additional information as needed, with special care not to overload low-altitude military fighters.

match.question_text: Under 10-2-10, what is the terminal facility notification path when an ELT signal is heard or reported?

replacements.question_text: When an ELT signal is heard or reported, what is the terminal facility notification path?
replacements.explanation: Terminal facilities notify the ARTCC, which then coordinates with the RCC. Terminal facilities also attempt to obtain fixes or bearings on the signal, solicit assistance of other aircraft known to be operating in the signal area, and forward fixes, bearings, and other pertinent information to the ARTCC.

choices.text: Notify the ARTCC, which coordinates with the RCC.
Notify the RCC directly and wait for the ARTCC to call later if needed.
Notify only technical operations unless another aircraft also hears the signal.
Notify flight service first and have them contact the ARTCC.

operations.source_basis: 10-2-10: 'b. TERMINAL. Notify the ARTCC which will coordinate with the RCC. c. TERMINAL. Attempt to obtain fixes or bearings on the signal. d. Solicit the assistance of other aircraft known to be operating in the signal area. e. TERMINAL. Forward fixes or bearings and any other pertinent information to the ARTCC. g. TERMINAL. content.situation: A terminal controller hears a strong ELT signal that appears to be coming from the airport area. Another controller wants to leave the position to search the field personally and says the ARTCC and technical operations can be notified after more precise bearings are collected.
content.para_context: Under 10-2-10, in terminal facilities notify the ARTCC, which coordinates with the RCC, attempt to obtain fixes or bearings, solicit assistance of other aircraft, and forward fixes, bearings, and other information to the ARTCC. If the signal appears to originate from an airport or vicinity, notify technical operations as well. Air traffic personnel must not leave required duty stations to locate an ELT signal source.

choices.text: Notify the ARTCC, obtain and forward fixes or bearings, notify technical operations if the source appears local to the airport, and remain at the duty station rather than leaving to search for the ELT.
Delay notification until enough bearings are collected to identify the source exactly because outside agencies should be called only after local confirmation.
Leave the position briefly to search the airport because locating the source is more important than remaining at the duty station during an ELT event.
Handle the event entirely within the terminal facility unless another aircraft reports hearing the signal too.

Do not suggest what actions the pilot should take concerning a bomb.

Keep the handling routine unless a bomb is actually found, because a reported threat alone does not justify emergency handling or outside technical assistance.

Eastern 417, exercise caution. MANPADS information will be on the next ATIS only.
All aircraft, MANPADS alert. Resume normal operations and advise when ready to copy the ATIS.

operations.problem: The para_context field opens with 'Under 10-2-13' (paragraph-location scaffolding). Explanation cites 'Per 10-2-13' and restates the answer without teaching the operational principles behind each required action.
operations.source_basis: 10-2-13a-d: Do not withhold landing clearance (a); disseminate via ATIS and/or controller-to-pilot (b); continue controller-to-pilot until ATIS acknowledged (c); continue until DEN ATSC directs otherwise (d).

content.situation: A MANPADS threat near the airport is determined to be real during arrivals. Another controller says to stop issuing landing clearance until the airline decides what it wants to do, and to stop controller-to-pilot advisories as soon as the ATIS is updated even if inbound crews have not yet acknowledged the current ATIS code.
content.para_context: Under 10-2-13, do not withhold landing clearance. Disseminate MANPADS information via the ATIS and/or controller-to-pilot transmissions. Continue controller-to-pilot transmissions until the appropriate MANPADS information is on the ATIS and pilots indicate they have received the appropriate ATIS code. Include the nature and location of the threat or incident, whether observed or reported and by whom, the time if known, and when transmitting to an individual aircraft, request the pilot's intentions. Continue until otherwise directed by the DEN Air Traffic Security Coordinator.

choices.text: Do not withhold landing clearance, keep controller-to-pilot advisories going until the information is on the ATIS and pilots report the appropriate ATIS code, and include the required threat details and a request for intentions when addressing an individual aircraft.
Suspend landing clearance until company approval is received, because operational decisions during a MANPADS event are made by ATC instead of the pilot or operator.
Stop all controller-to-pilot advisories as soon as the ATIS text is changed, even if the arriving aircraft have not yet acknowledged the updated ATIS code.
Provide only the existence of a threat and omit the location, reporting source, and time so the transmission stays short.

content.explanation: Per 10-2-13, landing clearance is not withheld, controller-to-pilot advisories continue until the ATIS information is both broadcast and acknowledged via the appropriate ATIS code, and the required threat details must be included.

content.situation: A MANPADS threat near the airport is determined to be real during arrivals. Continue until otherwise directed by the DEN Air Traffic Security Coordinator.

choices.text: Do not withhold landing clearance, keep controller-to-pilot advisories going until the information is on the ATIS and pilots report the appropriate ATIS code, and include the required threat details and a request for intentions when addressing an individual aircraft.
Suspend landing clearance until company approval is received, because operational decisions during a MANPADS event are made by ATC instead of the pilot or operator.
Stop all controller-to-pilot advisories as soon as the ATIS text is changed, even if the arriving aircraft have not yet acknowledged the updated ATIS code.

content.explanation: Landing clearance is not withheld during a MANPADS event. Controller-to-pilot advisories must continue until the ATIS information is both broadcast and acknowledged by pilots via the appropriate ATIS code. Threat details including nature, location, reporting source, and time must be included, and intentions must be requested from individual aircraft. Advisories continue until the DEN ATSC directs otherwise.

operations.source_basis: 10-2-16a lists five guidance methods, and 10-2-16b requires consideration of the 10-2-15 recommendation factors in conjunction with automated source information.

content.situation: A distressed pilot needs help reaching the recommended airport. Another controller says the only approved guidance method is radar vectors and that if radar coverage becomes unreliable, ATC has no other guidance options.
content.para_context: When necessary, guidance to the emergency airport may use radar, following another aircraft, NAVAIDs, pilotage by landmarks, or compass headings. Those methods still need to be used with the normal emergency-airport recommendation factors in mind.

choices.text: Use any suitable listed guidance method, including radar, following another aircraft, NAVAIDs, pilotage, or compass headings, while still considering the normal emergency-airport recommendation factors.
Use radar only, because the other methods are not approved for emergency guidance.
Stop providing guidance if radar becomes weak, because ATC may not shift to landmarks, headings, or NAVAIDs after recommending an airport.
Rely only on the automated emergency-airport tool after the airport is chosen, because controller judgment no longer applies during the guidance phase.

content.explanation: Per 10-2-16, controllers may use radar, another aircraft, NAVAIDs, pilotage, or compass headings to guide the aircraft, and those methods are still weighed with the usual emergency-airport recommendation factors.

content.situation: A distressed pilot needs help reaching the recommended airport. Another controller says the only approved guidance method is radar vectors and that if radar coverage becomes unreliable, ATC has no other guidance options.
content.para_context: When necessary, guidance to the emergency airport may use radar, following another aircraft, NAVAIDs, pilotage by landmarks, or compass headings. Those methods still need to be used with the normal emergency-airport recommendation factors in mind.

choices.text: Use any suitable listed guidance method, including radar, following another aircraft, NAVAIDs, pilotage, or compass headings, while still considering the normal emergency-airport recommendation factors.
Use radar only, because the other methods are not approved for emergency guidance.
Stop providing guidance if radar becomes weak, because ATC may not shift to landmarks, headings, or NAVAIDs after recommending an airport.
Rely only on the automated emergency-airport tool after the airport is chosen, because controller judgment no longer applies during the guidance phase.

content.explanation: Controllers may use radar, following another aircraft, NAVAIDs, pilotage by landmarks, or compass headings to guide an aircraft to an emergency airport. These guidance methods remain available regardless of which method is used initially, and they are still weighed together with the usual emergency-airport recommendation factors.

operations.problem: The explanation opens with 'Per 10-2-17' — paragraph-location scaffolding. The correct choice is substantially longer than the distractors, creating an answer-length cue.
operations.source_basis: 10-2-17a states the EOVM/EAM must only be used when the pilot has declared an emergency or the controller has determined that an emergency condition exists or is imminent because the pilot cannot maintain an appropriate terrain/obstacle clearance minimum altitude.

content.question_text: When may the EOVM or EAM be used to provide emergency advisory service?

choices.text: Only when the pilot has declared an emergency or the controller determines an emergency exists or is imminent because the aircraft cannot maintain an appropriate terrain or obstacle clearance minimum altitude.
Any time a pilot requests vectors around weather, even if terrain clearance is not an issue.
Only after the aircraft has descended below the MVA and radar contact is lost.
Whenever an automated airport-selection tool recommends a lower terrain route.

content.explanation: Per 10-2-17, the EOVM/EAM is used only when the pilot has declared an emergency or the controller determines an emergency exists or is imminent because an appropriate terrain or obstacle clearance minimum altitude cannot be maintained.

content.question_text: When may the EOVM or EAM be used to provide emergency advisory service?

choices.text: Only when the pilot has declared an emergency or the controller determines an emergency exists or is imminent because the aircraft cannot maintain an appropriate terrain or obstacle clearance minimum altitude.
Any time a pilot requests vectors around weather, even if no emergency exists and terrain or obstacle clearance is not at risk.
Only after the aircraft has descended below the MVA and radar contact has been lost and the pilot cannot re-establish communications.
Whenever an automated airport-selection tool recommends a lower terrain route regardless of whether an emergency has been declared or determined.

content.explanation: The EOVM and EAM are used only when the pilot has declared an emergency or the controller determines an emergency exists or is imminent because an appropriate terrain or obstacle clearance minimum altitude cannot be maintained. Appropriate minimum altitudes include MIA, MEA, MOCA, and MVA.

operations.problem: The para_context opens with 'Under 10-2-17' and the explanation opens with 'Per 10-2-17' — paragraph-location scaffolding in both fields. The activity scenario is otherwise well-constructed, testing both the precondition for EOVM/EAM use and the required pilot advisory.
operations.source_basis: 10-2-17 requires a declared or controller-determined emergency involving inability to maintain terrain/obstacle clearance as a precondition, and requires the controller to advise the pilot that headings are emergency advisories intended only to direct toward lower terrain/obstacle elevation. The EOVM contains no lateral or vertical buffers.

content.situation: A pilot is descending below safe terrain clearance in deteriorating weather and cannot maintain an appropriate minimum altitude. Another controller wants to start using the EOVM immediately without treating the situation as an emergency and without telling the pilot that the headings are only emergency advisories toward lower terrain.
content.para_context: Under 10-2-17, the EOVM/EAM may be used only when the pilot has declared an emergency or when the controller determines an emergency condition exists or is imminent because of the pilot's inability to maintain an appropriate terrain or obstacle clearance minimum altitude. When providing emergency vectoring service, the controller must advise the pilot that headings issued are emergency advisories intended only to direct the aircraft toward and over an area of lower terrain or obstacle elevation. The map shows actual altitudes and obstacle locations and contains no lateral or vertical obstruction-clearance buffers.

choices.text: Use the EOVM/EAM only once the emergency-use condition is met, and tell the pilot the headings are emergency advisories intended only to direct the aircraft toward lower terrain or obstacle elevation.
Use the EOVM whenever a pilot asks for vectors, even if no emergency exists and safe terrain clearance is not an issue.
Issue EOVM headings as if they guarantee normal obstruction clearance buffers, so there is no need to qualify the service.
Delay all vectors until the pilot can quote the exact MVA or MEA, because controller determination of an imminent emergency is not enough to use the map.

content.explanation: Per 10-2-17, EOVM/EAM use is limited to declared or controller-determined emergency terrain-clearance situations, and the pilot must be told that the headings are emergency advisories only toward lower terrain or obstacle elevation.

content.situation: A pilot is descending below safe terrain clearance in deteriorating weather and cannot maintain an appropriate minimum altitude. Another controller wants to start using the EOVM immediately without treating the situation as an emergency and without telling the pilot that the headings are only emergency advisories toward lower terrain.
content.para_context: The EOVM and EAM may be used only when the pilot has declared an emergency or when the controller determines an emergency condition exists or is imminent because of the pilot's inability to maintain an appropriate terrain or obstacle clearance minimum altitude. When providing emergency vectoring service, the controller must advise the pilot that headings issued are emergency advisories intended only to direct the aircraft toward and over an area of lower terrain or obstacle elevation. The map shows actual altitudes and obstacle locations and contains no lateral or vertical obstruction-clearance buffers.

choices.text: Use the EOVM or EAM only once the emergency-use condition is met, and tell the pilot the headings are emergency advisories intended only to direct the aircraft toward lower terrain or obstacle elevation.
Use the EOVM whenever a pilot asks for vectors, even if no emergency exists and safe terrain clearance is not an issue.
Issue EOVM headings as if they guarantee normal obstruction clearance buffers, so there is no need to qualify the service.
Delay all vectors until the pilot can quote the exact MVA or MEA, because controller determination of an imminent emergency is not enough to use the map.

content.explanation: EOVM and EAM use is limited to declared or controller-determined emergency terrain-clearance situations. When the condition is met, the pilot must be told that the headings are emergency advisories only intended to direct the aircraft toward lower terrain or obstacle elevation. The map has no obstruction-clearance buffers, so this advisory is essential.

operations.problem: The explanation opens with 'Per 10-2-18' — paragraph-location scaffolding in explanation. The negative formulation of the stem ('what should ATC avoid') correctly tests the prohibition as stated in the source.
operations.source_basis: 10-2-18b2 states do not initiate any climb clearances to turbine-powered aircraft until the aircraft has exited the ash cloud.

content.question_text: If a turbine-powered aircraft reports it has entered a volcanic ash cloud and a distress situation exists, what should ATC avoid initiating until the aircraft exits the ash cloud?

choices.text: Climb clearances.
Any exchange of position information.
Any descent planning, even if terrain requires it.
Any coordination with adjacent facilities.

content.explanation: Per 10-2-18, climb clearances are not initiated for turbine-powered aircraft until the aircraft has exited the volcanic ash cloud.

content.question_text: If a turbine-powered aircraft reports it has entered a volcanic ash cloud and a distress situation exists, what should ATC avoid initiating until the aircraft exits the ash cloud?

choices.text: Climb clearances.
Any exchange of position information.
Any descent planning, even if terrain requires it.
Any coordination with adjacent facilities.

content.explanation: Do not initiate any climb clearances to turbine-powered aircraft until the aircraft has exited the volcanic ash cloud. Controllers should be aware of the possibility of complete loss of power to any turbine-powered aircraft that encounters an ash cloud.

operations.problem: The back of the card invents content not present in the source: 'airport suitability and facilities,' 'length and surface,' 'approach aids,' 'rescue services,' and 'any other factor relevant to the emergency.' None of these terms appear in the 10-2-15 source factors. The card also omits three source factors: aircraft type, pilot qualifications, and vectoring or homing capability. This teaches incorrect content that an examiner would mark wrong.
operations.source_basis: 10-2-15a lists seven factors: 1. Remaining fuel in relation to airport distances. 2. Weather conditions. 3. Airport conditions. 4. NAVAID status. 5. Aircraft type. 6. Pilot's qualifications. 7. Vectoring or homing capability to the emergency airport.

match.front: What factors should be considered when recommending an emergency airport?
match.back: Consider fuel remaining, weather, airport suitability and facilities, length and surface, approach aids, rescue services, and any other factor relevant to the emergency.

replacements.front: What factors must a controller consider when recommending an emergency airport?
replacements.back: Remaining fuel in relation to airport distances; weather conditions; airport conditions; NAVAID status; aircraft type; pilot's qualifications; and vectoring or homing capability to the emergency airport.

operations.source_basis: When an air traffic controller receives a report of the death of a person, an illness, or other public health risk, obtain the following information: call sign, number of suspected cases of illness, nature of the illnesses or other public health risk if known, number of persons on board, number of deaths if applicable, pilot's intent, and any request for assistance.

match.question_text: Under 10-2-19, which item is one of the required pieces of information to obtain from a pilot reporting illness, death, or another public health risk on board?

replacements.question_text: Which of the following is one of the required pieces of information a controller must obtain from a pilot reporting illness, death, or another public health risk on board?
replacements.explanation: When a controller receives a report of death, illness, or other public health risk, the required information includes: call sign, number of suspected illness cases, nature of the illness or risk if known, number of persons on board, number of deaths if applicable, pilot's intent, and any request for assistance.

choices.text: Pilot's intent, such as continuing to destination or diverting.
The aircraft's assigned altitude at the time of the report.
The aircraft operator's home station contact number.
Whether customs services are needed after landing.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 10-2-19' — paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation references '10-2-19b' — paragraph-location scaffolding in explanation. All three distractors begin with 'Only...' while the correct answer does not, creating a systematic answer-wording cue.

replacements.question_text: After a controller obtains public-health information from a pilot, who must relay that information to the DEN as soon as possible?
replacements.explanation: The OMIC, OS, or CIC must relay the collected public-health information to the DEN as soon as possible after receiving it from the controller.

choices.text: The OMIC, OS, or CIC.
The pilot in command after landing.
The receiving sector after handoff is complete.
The local airport manager.

operations.source_basis: If the ATC facility is not actively monitoring the DEN or does not have a dedicated line to the DEN, they must call into the DEN directly.

match.question_text: Under the note in 10-2-19, if the ATC facility is not actively monitoring the DEN or does not have a dedicated line, it must call the DEN directly via 844-432-2962.

replacements.question_text: What must an ATC facility do if it is not actively monitoring the DEN and does not have a dedicated line to the DEN when it receives a public-health report?
replacements.explanation: When an ATC facility is not actively monitoring the DEN or lacks a dedicated line to the DEN, it must call the DEN directly rather than waiting for contact through other channels.

choices.text: Call the DEN directly.
Wait for the DEN to initiate contact with the facility.
Route the information through the next sector for relay to the DEN.
File a written report for delivery within 24 hours.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under the notes in 10-2-19' — paragraph-location scaffolding. The explanation 'That limitation appears in note 2 under 10-2-19' is thin; it cites a paragraph location instead of teaching the operational rule that follow-on coordination moves off ATC frequencies.
operations.source_basis: Except in extraordinary circumstances, such as a situation requiring ATC intervention, follow-on coordination regarding the incident will not involve ATC frequencies.

replacements.explanation: Follow-on coordination regarding the public-health incident will not involve ATC frequencies, except in extraordinary circumstances requiring ATC intervention. choices.text: True
False

replacements.explanation: It may be best to keep the aircraft on the initial contact frequency rather than moving to a designated emergency frequency. Change frequencies only when there is a valid reason to do so.

choices.text: Keep the aircraft on the initial contact frequency.
Move the aircraft to 121.5 immediately in every case.

choices.text: Only when there is a valid reason.
Whenever an emergency aircraft checks in on any frequency other than 121.5.

Changing the aircraft to a discrete transponder code as the primary orientation method.

operations.source_basis: When a report of death, illness, or other public health risk is received, obtain the required information and notify the OMIC, OS, or CIC as soon as possible. The OMIC, OS, or CIC must relay the information to the DEN as soon as possible.

content.question_text: After receiving a report of illness, death, or another public-health risk on board, what should the controller do as soon as possible?

choices.text: Obtain the listed information and notify the OMIC, OS, or CIC as soon as possible.
Wait until the flight lands before collecting any details so the crew can focus on flying.
Coordinate follow-on public-health details over ATC frequencies whenever possible.
Relay only the call sign to the next sector and let that sector handle the rest.

content.explanation: Per 10-2-19, the controller obtains the listed information and notifies the OMIC, OS, or CIC as soon as possible so it can be relayed to the DEN.

content.question_text: After receiving a report of illness, death, or another public-health risk on board, what should the controller do as soon as possible?

choices.text: Obtain the listed information and notify the OMIC, OS, or CIC as soon as possible.
Wait until the flight lands before collecting any details so the crew can focus on flying.
Coordinate follow-on public-health details over ATC frequencies whenever possible.
Relay only the call sign to the next sector and let that sector handle the rest.

content.explanation: The controller must obtain the public-health information — call sign, number of suspected illness cases, nature of illness or risk if known, number of persons on board, number of deaths if applicable, pilot's intent, and any request for assistance — and notify the OMIC, OS, or CIC as soon as possible. The OMIC, OS, or CIC then relays the information to the DEN.

operations.source_basis: When a report of death, illness, or other public health risk is received, obtain the call sign, number of suspected illness cases, nature of illness or public health risk if known, number of persons on board, number of deaths if applicable, pilot's intent, and any request for assistance, then notify the OMIC, OS, or CIC as soon as possible. The OMIC, OS, or CIC must relay the information to the DEN as soon as possible.

content.situation: A pilot reports several ill passengers and requests medical assistance on arrival. Another controller wants to pass the flight to the next sector first and collect only the callsign and destination, saying public-health details can wait until after landing.
content.para_context: Under 10-2-19, when a report of death, illness, or other public health risk is received, obtain the call sign, number of suspected illness cases, nature of illness or public health risk if known, number of persons on board, number of deaths if applicable, pilot's intent, and any request for assistance, then notify the OMIC/OS/CIC as soon as possible. The OMIC/OS/CIC must relay the information to the DEN as soon as possible.

choices.text: Obtain the listed public-health details, including cases, nature of illness, persons on board, deaths if any, pilot intent, and assistance requested, then notify the OMIC/OS/CIC as soon as possible.
Collect only the callsign and destination first, because the rest of the public-health details can wait until the flight lands and taxis in.
Handle the follow-on coordination entirely on ATC frequency with outside agencies so everyone hears the same details immediately.
Wait until the next sector takes handoff responsibility, because only the destination facility is allowed to start public-health reporting.

content.explanation: Per 10-2-19, collect the listed information promptly and notify the OMIC/OS/CIC as soon as possible so the information can be relayed to the DEN.

content.situation: A pilot reports several ill passengers and requests medical assistance on arrival. Another controller wants to pass the flight to the next sector first and collect only the callsign and destination, saying public-health details can wait until after landing.

choices.text: Obtain the listed public-health details, including cases, nature of illness, persons on board, deaths if any, pilot intent, and assistance requested, then notify the OMIC, OS, or CIC as soon as possible.

Handle the follow-on coordination entirely on ATC frequency with outside agencies so everyone hears the same details immediately.

content.explanation: Collect the listed public-health information promptly and notify the OMIC, OS, or CIC as soon as possible so the information can be relayed to the DEN. The controller must obtain the required items of information at the time of the report, not defer collection to a later sector or to after landing.

replacements.question_text: What transponder code does ATC assign when a pilot verbally notifies of a hijacking situation?
replacements.explanation: Code 7500 is the transponder code for unlawful interference (hijacking). When a pilot verbally notifies ATC of a hijacking situation, the controller assigns code 7500 to the subject aircraft. Code 7600 indicates radio failure and code 7700 indicates a general emergency — neither is the correct response to a hijack notification.

operations.source_basis: Consider an aircraft to be overdue and initiate the procedures stated in this section to issue an ALNOT when neither communications nor radar contact can be established and 30 minutes have passed since: 1. Its ETA over a specified or compulsory reporting point or at a clearance limit in your area. 2. Its clearance void time. 3. A VFR or IFR aircraft arriving at an airport not served by an air traffic control tower or flight service station fails to cancel a flight plan after receiving instructions on how to cancel.

replacements.explanation: An aircraft is considered overdue for ALNOT purposes when neither communications nor radar contact can be established and 30 minutes have passed since: its ETA over a specified or compulsory reporting point or at a clearance limit; its clearance void time; or, for an arriving aircraft at an airport not served by a tower or FSS, its failure to cancel a flight plan after receiving cancellation instructions. match.question_text: Under 10-3-4, before the RCC can begin search and rescue procedures, what must happen?

replacements.question_text: Before the Rescue Coordination Center can begin search and rescue procedures for an overdue aircraft, what must happen?
replacements.explanation: An Alert Notice (ALNOT) must be issued before the RCC can begin search and rescue procedures. The ALNOT distributes overdue-aircraft information to all centers and Area B circuits along the route of flight, and its issuance is a prerequisite that enables the RCC to initiate formal SAR operations.

choices.text: An ALNOT must be issued.
The destination FSS must close the flight plan as overdue.
The aircraft fuel exhaustion time must be exceeded by 30 minutes.
A second center must independently confirm the aircraft is missing.

The explanation uses 'Per 10-3-4a' scaffolding.
operations.source_basis: 10-3-4a states: 'issue an ALNOT to all centers and Area B circuits, generally 50 miles on either side of the route of flight from the last reported position to destination.' This defines the normal ALNOT distribution area.

match.question_text: Under 10-3-4, what is the normal ALNOT distribution area from the last reported position to destination?

replacements.question_text: When issuing an ALNOT for an overdue aircraft, what is the normal distribution area from the last reported position to the destination?
replacements.explanation: The ALNOT must be issued to all centers and Area B circuits along the route of flight, generally covering 50 miles on either side of the route from the last reported position to destination. At the recommendation of the RCC or at the controller's discretion, the ALNOT may be expanded to cover the maximum range of the aircraft.

choices.text: All centers and Area B circuits, generally 50 miles on either side of the route of flight.
Only the destination center, with no lateral route-of-flight distribution required.
All centers and Area B circuits within 100 miles of the departure airport only.
Only Area B circuits within 10 miles of the last reported position, regardless of destination.

replacements.explanation: When information is obtained that was not previously contained in the ALNOT, an amended ALNOT must be issued to update the information. The amended ALNOT distributes the new data to the same recipients as the original alert, giving SAR providers the most current information available for their search efforts.

Cancel the original ALNOT and file a fresh flight plan for the aircraft.

operations.source_basis: 10-3-4b states: 'Upon receipt of an INREQ or ALNOT, check the position records to determine whether the aircraft has contacted your facility. Notify the originator of the results or status of this check within one hour of the time the alert was received. Retain the alert in an active status, and immediately notify the originator of subsequent contact, until cancellation is received.'

replacements.explanation: Upon receiving an INREQ or ALNOT, the facility must check its position records and notify the originator of the results or status of the check within one hour of the time the alert was received. The facility must also retain the alert in active status and immediately notify the originator of any subsequent contact with the aircraft, keeping the alert active until cancellation is received.

choices.text: Within one hour of the time the alert was received.
Within 30 minutes of the time the alert was received.
By the end of the current shift, regardless of when the alert was received.
Only after the RCC specifically requests a status update on the alert.

Send the ALNOT only to the destination center because the wider Area B and route distribution is optional unless the aircraft is military.

Retain the alert in active status, and amend the ALNOT when new information is obtained that will assist SAR providers.

Send the ALNOT only to the destination center because the wider Area B and route distribution is optional unless the aircraft is military.

operations.source_basis: 10-3-5 lists three independent conditions for transferring search responsibility to the RCC, including: 'The aircraft has not been located within one hour after ALNOT issuance.' Any one of the three conditions is sufficient to trigger the transfer.

match.question_text: Under 10-3-5, which condition transfers responsibility for further search to the RCC?

replacements.question_text: An ALNOT was issued for an overdue aircraft. One hour has passed and the aircraft has not been located. What must happen with search responsibility?
replacements.explanation: Responsibility for further search transfers to the RCC when any one of three conditions occurs. One of those conditions is that the aircraft has not been located within one hour after ALNOT issuance. The other two triggers are: 30 minutes have elapsed after estimated fuel exhaustion time, or the ALNOT search has been completed with negative results. Only one condition needs to be met.

choices.text: Transfer responsibility for further search to the RCC.
Continue the center's search indefinitely until the destination airport closes.
Wait for all three transfer conditions to occur before transferring responsibility.
Transfer responsibility only after two hours have elapsed beyond estimated fuel exhaustion time.

operations.problem: The stem uses 'Under 10-3-5' paragraph-location scaffolding. The operational concept — testing whether a controller understands that the three transfer triggers are independent rather than cumulative — is educationally important. The explanation correctly identifies the independent-trigger structure but could teach the principle more clearly.
operations.source_basis: 10-3-5 states: 'Transfer responsibility for further search to the RCC when one of the following occurs.' The phrase 'one of the following' establishes that any single condition triggers the transfer; all three conditions are not required.

match.question_text: Under 10-3-5, responsibility transfers only after all listed trigger conditions have occurred.

replacements.question_text: Responsibility for further search transfers to the RCC only after all three transfer conditions have occurred: fuel exhaustion time plus 30 minutes, one hour after ALNOT without locating the aircraft, and a completed ALNOT search with negative results.
replacements.explanation: Responsibility transfers to the RCC when any ONE of the three conditions occurs — not all three. The source states 'when one of the following occurs,' making each condition an independent trigger. Requiring all three would delay the transfer and keep search responsibility at the center long after any single trigger had been met.

False

The format tests recognition of a restated source sentence rather than operational application. With items 581436bb (MC covering the 1-hour-after-ALNOT trigger) and 4c2c8c57 (T/F testing any-one-vs-all), the fuel-exhaustion trigger deserves a comparable applied format.
operations.source_basis: 10-3-5a states: 'Thirty minutes have elapsed after the estimated aircraft fuel exhaustion time.' This is one of three independent conditions that transfer search responsibility to the RCC.

replacements.question_text: The estimated fuel exhaustion time for an overdue aircraft was 1430Z. The ALNOT search is still in progress and the aircraft has not been located. Based on the fuel exhaustion trigger alone, at what time must responsibility for further search transfer to the RCC?
replacements.explanation: When 30 minutes have elapsed after the estimated aircraft fuel exhaustion time, responsibility transfers to the RCC. With an estimated exhaustion time of 1430Z, the transfer must occur at 1500Z. This trigger is independent of the other two conditions; the transfer does not wait for the ALNOT search to complete or for the one-hour-after-ALNOT threshold to be reached.

Keep responsibility at the center until all three transfer conditions have occurred, because the RCC takes over only after every threshold is met.

There is no requirement to wait for fuel exhaustion time to also pass or for all three conditions to be met.

replacements.explanation: To prevent undue delay, use any available method to determine the initial latitude and longitude immediately, then follow up as soon as possible with the formal coordinates from the appropriate facility data extraction tools.

operations.problem: The para_context opens with 'Under 10-4-4' and the explanation opens with 'Per 10-4-4,' both teaching paragraph-location trivia instead of providing operational context for the lost-communications procedure.
operations.source_basis: In the event of lost communications with an aircraft under your control jurisdiction use all appropriate means available to reestablish communications with the aircraft. If radio communications have not been (re) established with the aircraft after 5 minutes, consider the aircraft's or pilot's activity to be suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC.

content.situation: You lose two-way radio communications with an IFR aircraft under your control jurisdiction. Another controller wants to stop after waiting for Code 7600, saying there is no reason to try emergency frequencies, voice-capable NAVAIDs, relays, or transponder/turn acknowledgments unless the pilot first makes contact.
content.para_context: Under 10-4-4, use all appropriate means available to reestablish communications, including emergency frequencies, voice-capable NAVAIDs, FSS, New York Radio, San Francisco Radio, ACARS/SELCAL when suitable, broadcast clearances through any available means, and attempt acknowledgment through transponder replies or turns. If communications are not reestablished after 5 minutes, consider the activity suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC.

choices.text: Use all appropriate means to reestablish communications, including relays and transponder/turn acknowledgments, and if still not reestablished after 5 minutes, treat the activity as suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC.
Wait for the pilot to select Code 7600 and then take no further action unless the aircraft declares an emergency.
Use only emergency frequency 121.5 because voice NAVAIDs, FSS relays, and radio networks are outside normal ATC tools.
Delay all follow-up action until at least 30 minutes have elapsed, because suspicious-activity reporting does not apply to lost communications.

content.explanation: Per 10-4-4, controllers use all appropriate means to reestablish communications, may use transponder or turn acknowledgments, and report suspicious activity to the OS/CIC if communications have not been reestablished after 5 minutes.

content.situation: You lose two-way radio communications with an IFR aircraft under your control jurisdiction. Another controller wants to stop after waiting for Code 7600, saying there is no reason to try emergency frequencies, voice-capable NAVAIDs, relays, or transponder/turn acknowledgments unless the pilot first makes contact.
content.para_context: When communications are lost, use all appropriate means available to reestablish communications, including emergency frequencies, voice-capable NAVAIDs, FSS, New York Radio, San Francisco Radio, ACARS/SELCAL when suitable, broadcast clearances through any available means, and attempt acknowledgment through transponder replies or turns. If communications are not reestablished after 5 minutes, consider the activity suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC.

choices.text: Use all appropriate means to reestablish communications, including relays and transponder/turn acknowledgments, and if still not reestablished after 5 minutes, treat the activity as suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC.
Wait for the pilot to select Code 7600 and then take no further action unless the aircraft declares an emergency.
Use only emergency frequency 121.5 because voice NAVAIDs, FSS relays, and radio networks are outside normal ATC tools.
Delay all follow-up action until at least 30 minutes have elapsed, because suspicious-activity reporting does not apply to lost communications.

content.explanation: Controllers must use all appropriate means to reestablish communications, may use transponder or turn acknowledgments, and must report suspicious activity to the OS/CIC if communications have not been reestablished after 5 minutes.

operations.source_basis: A debris-generating space launch or reentry vehicle mishap is an emergency situation in the NAS. Issue an alert broadcast to all affected aircraft. Prevent aircraft from entering the debris response area. Assist aircraft to exit the DRA expeditiously. Do not withhold landing or approach clearances. For airports that underlie an active DRA do not issue departure releases or takeoff clearances and to the extent possible do not clear aircraft onto the movement area.

content.situation: A debris response area has just been activated for a space vehicle mishap. You have airborne aircraft already inside the DRA, more departures ready from an airport under the area, and another controller wants to hold the arrivals outside while continuing normal departures because the debris warning has already been broadcast.
content.para_context: Under 10-5-2, a debris-generating space launch or reentry vehicle mishap is an emergency situation in the NAS. Issue alert broadcasts to affected aircraft, prevent aircraft from entering an activated DRA unless a higher priority duty exists, assist airborne aircraft already inside to exit expeditiously, do not withhold landing or approach clearances, and for airports underlying an active DRA do not issue departure releases or takeoff clearances and, to the extent possible, do not clear aircraft onto the movement area.

choices.text: Keep aircraft from entering the DRA, help aircraft already inside exit expeditiously, do not withhold landing or approach clearances, and stop departure releases and takeoff clearances for airports underlying the active DRA.
Continue normal departures because the alert broadcast satisfies the ATC response, and hold arrivals outside until the DRA is canceled.
Vector all aircraft deeper into the DRA to maintain orderly flow, because debris avoidance is the pilots' responsibility once notified.
Withhold all landing clearances for aircraft already inside the DRA until every departure has been stopped and the area is empty.

content.explanation: Per 10-5-2, a DRA activation requires protecting aircraft from entering, expediting aircraft out of the area, continuing landing/approach clearances as specified, and stopping departures from airports that underlie the active DRA.

choices.text: Keep aircraft from entering the DRA, help aircraft already inside exit expeditiously, do not withhold landing or approach clearances, and stop departure releases and takeoff clearances for airports underlying the active DRA.

operations.source_basis: 10-6-2a1 states that an INCERFA exists when communication from an aircraft has not been received within 30 minutes after the time a communication should have been received or after the time an unsuccessful attempt to establish communication with such aircraft was first made, whichever is earlier. INCERFA reflects concern about the safety of an aircraft or its occupants.

replacements.question_text: Which emergency phase exists when communication from an aircraft has not been received within 30 minutes after it should have been received, or after the first unsuccessful attempt to establish communication, whichever is earlier?
replacements.explanation: The uncertainty phase (INCERFA) exists when there is concern about the safety of an aircraft or its occupants. One INCERFA trigger is a 30-minute communication gap — when communication has not been received within 30 minutes after it should have been received, or after the first unsuccessful attempt to establish communication, whichever is earlier. This is the lowest emergency phase threshold.

ALERFA.
DETRESFA.

operations.source_basis: 10-6-3b states that unless otherwise established by letter of agreement, responsibility for coordinating alerting service rests with the facility serving the FIR or CTA: (1) within which the aircraft was flying at the time of last air-ground radio contact; or (2) which the aircraft was about to enter if the last air-ground contact was established at or close to the boundary; or (3) within which the point of destination is located if the aircraft was not equipped with suitable two-way radio communications equipment or was not under obligation to transmit position reports.

replacements.question_text: Absent a letter of agreement to the contrary, which facility is responsible for coordinating alerting service?
replacements.explanation: Alerting-service coordination rests with the facility serving the FIR or CTA where the aircraft was flying at the time of last air-ground radio contact. If that last contact was established at or close to a FIR boundary, the facility serving the FIR or CTA the aircraft was about to enter assumes responsibility. For aircraft not equipped with suitable two-way radio or not under obligation to transmit position reports, the facility where the destination is located is responsible.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-6-4' which is paragraph-location scaffolding. The explanation references 'Per 10-6-4b' rather than teaching the respective roles of the RCC and ACC in SAR airspace management during ditching.
operations.source_basis: 10-6-4b states that in all cases of aircraft ditching, the airspace required for SAR operations must be determined by the RCC. The ACC must block that airspace until the RCC advises the airspace is no longer required. An International NOTAM must be issued describing the airspace affected.

replacements.question_text: In all cases of aircraft ditching, who determines the airspace required for SAR operations, and what must the ACC do with that airspace?
replacements.explanation: The RCC determines the airspace required for SAR operations. The ACC must block that airspace until the RCC advises it is no longer required, and an International NOTAM must be issued describing the affected airspace. The RCC decides the scope; the ACC enforces the block.

choices.text: The RCC determines the required SAR airspace, and the ACC blocks it until the RCC advises it is no longer required.
The ACC determines the SAR airspace, and the RCC only requests a NOTAM after rescue is complete.
The distressed aircraft determines the SAR airspace, and the ACC keeps it open to regular traffic if pilots concur.
The nearest tower determines the SAR airspace, and the ACC blocks it only for 30 minutes unless renewed.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-6-5' which is paragraph-location scaffolding. The explanation references 'Per 10-6-5b' rather than teaching the reason for the fixed-clearance-limit practice (the distressed aircraft is a moving fix) and the route-consistency requirement.
operations.source_basis: 10-6-5b states to clear the SAR aircraft to a fixed clearance limit rather than to the aircraft in distress, which is a moving fix. Issue route clearances that are consistent with that of the distressed aircraft.

match.question_text: Under 10-6-5, what clearance-limit practice applies to the SAR aircraft?

replacements.question_text: When clearing a SAR aircraft responding to an aircraft in distress, what clearance-limit practice applies?
replacements.explanation: The SAR aircraft is cleared to a fixed clearance limit rather than to the aircraft in distress, which is a moving fix. Route clearances issued to the SAR aircraft must be consistent with the route of the distressed aircraft.

choices.text: Clear it to a fixed clearance limit rather than to the aircraft in distress.
Clear it directly to the aircraft in distress as a moving fix.
Give no clearance limit once the RCC activates the SAR mission.
Use only a holding fix near the operator's home base.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-6-5' which is paragraph-location scaffolding. The explanation references 'Per 10-6-5a' rather than teaching that IFR separation is the default and the two-part concurrence condition required to discontinue it (SAR aircraft has visual or radar contact and both pilots concur).
operations.source_basis: 10-6-5a states to provide IFR separation between the SAR and the aircraft in distress, except when visual or radar contact has been established by the search and rescue aircraft and the pilots of both aircraft concur, IFR separation may be discontinued.

match.question_text: Under 10-6-5, when may IFR separation between the SAR aircraft and the aircraft in distress be discontinued?

replacements.question_text: When may IFR separation between a SAR aircraft and the aircraft in distress be discontinued?
replacements.explanation: IFR separation between the SAR aircraft and the distressed aircraft is required by default. It may be discontinued only when visual or radar contact has been established by the SAR aircraft and the pilots of both aircraft concur. Both conditions — contact and concurrence — must be satisfied.

choices.text: When the SAR aircraft has visual or radar contact and the pilots of both aircraft concur.
Whenever the rescue aircraft reports the distressed aircraft on TCAS.
Only after the distressed aircraft lands successfully.
Whenever the RCC requests it, even if neither aircraft has contact with the other.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 10-6-5' which is paragraph-location scaffolding. The explanation says only 'That instruction appears in 10-6-5c' which is a paragraph citation placeholder with zero instructional value — it does not teach what kinds of factors must be communicated or when.
operations.source_basis: 10-6-5c states to advise the rescue aircraft, as soon as practicable, of any factors that could adversely affect its mission; examples include unfavorable weather conditions, anticipated problems, and the possibility of not being able to approve an IFR descent through en route traffic.

match.question_text: Under 10-6-5, the rescue aircraft should be advised as soon as practicable of factors that could adversely affect its mission.

replacements.question_text: The rescue aircraft should be advised as soon as practicable of any factors that could adversely affect its mission.
replacements.explanation: Controllers must advise the rescue aircraft as soon as practicable of factors that could adversely affect its mission, such as unfavorable weather conditions, anticipated problems, or the possibility of being unable to approve an IFR descent through en route traffic.

operations.problem: The para_context opens with 'Under 10-6-2' and contains 'in the paragraph' — both are paragraph-location scaffolding and a generic document reference. The explanation opens with 'Per 10-6-2' which is a paragraph citation instead of teaching the phase threshold logic. The activity itself and its choices are substantively sound.
operations.source_basis: 10-6-2 defines three escalating emergency phases: INCERFA exists when there is concern about safety (30-minute missed-communication or missed-arrival threshold); ALERFA exists when there is apprehension (requires unsuccessful follow-up, impaired operating efficiency, or 60-minute communication gap); DETRESFA exists when there is reasonable certainty of grave and imminent danger (unsuccessful ALERFA follow-up, fuel exhaustion, impaired efficiency to forced-landing extent, or actual/impending forced landing).

content.situation: An oceanic aircraft has not been heard from for 35 minutes after a report should have been received. No other information suggests fuel exhaustion, forced landing, or severe impairment. Another controller wants to jump immediately to DETRESFA because any missed oceanic report should be treated as grave and imminent danger.
content.para_context: Under 10-6-2, an INCERFA exists when communication has not been received within 30 minutes after it should have been received or when an aircraft fails to arrive within 30 minutes after the latest estimated arrival time. ALERFA and DETRESFA apply only when the higher thresholds or more serious facts in the paragraph are present.

choices.text: Treat the situation as INCERFA because the 30-minute communication threshold has been crossed, but the facts do not yet establish ALERFA or DETRESFA.
Declare DETRESFA immediately because every missed oceanic communication report means grave and imminent danger.
Wait until 60 minutes have passed before recognizing any emergency phase at all.
Treat it as ALERFA solely because communications were missed by more than 30 minutes, regardless of the phase definitions.

content.explanation: Per 10-6-2, the 30-minute missed-communication threshold establishes INCERFA absent the additional facts that would support ALERFA or DETRESFA.

content.situation: An oceanic aircraft has not been heard from for 35 minutes after a report should have been received. No other information suggests fuel exhaustion, forced landing, or severe impairment. Another controller wants to jump immediately to DETRESFA because any missed oceanic report should be treated as grave and imminent danger.
content.para_context: An INCERFA exists when communication has not been received within 30 minutes after it should have been received, or when an aircraft fails to arrive within 30 minutes after the latest estimated arrival time. ALERFA requires additional conditions such as unsuccessful follow-up inquiries or information indicating impaired operating efficiency. DETRESFA requires reasonable certainty that the aircraft and its occupants are threatened by grave and imminent danger.

choices.text: Treat the situation as INCERFA because the 30-minute communication threshold has been crossed, but the facts do not yet establish ALERFA or DETRESFA.
Declare DETRESFA immediately because every missed oceanic communication report means grave and imminent danger.
Wait until 60 minutes have passed before recognizing any emergency phase at all.
Treat it as ALERFA solely because communications were missed by more than 30 minutes, regardless of the phase definitions.

content.explanation: The 30-minute missed-communication threshold establishes INCERFA. Absent the additional facts that would support ALERFA (unsuccessful follow-up inquiries, impaired operating efficiency, or a 60-minute gap) or DETRESFA (fuel exhaustion, forced landing, or reasonable certainty of grave and imminent danger), the higher phases are not yet triggered.

operations.problem: The para_context opens with 'Under 10-6-3' which is paragraph-location scaffolding. The explanation opens with 'Per 10-6-3' which is a paragraph citation instead of teaching the FIR/CTA coordination responsibility and ACC control-point role.
operations.source_basis: 10-6-3b states that unless otherwise established by LOA, alerting-service coordination rests with the facility serving the FIR or CTA within which the aircraft was flying at last air-ground contact, or which the aircraft was about to enter if contact was near the boundary, or where the destination is located for non-communicating aircraft. 10-6-3c states the responsible ACC serves as the control point for collecting information, forwarding it to the RCC, and coordinating with other facilities.

content.situation: An aircraft in oceanic operations becomes subject to alerting service just as it approaches the boundary of the next FIR. Another controller wants the destination FIR to take the entire coordination burden immediately because the destination is already known, while nobody has yet notified the RCC or the operator.
content.para_context: Under 10-6-3, unless otherwise established by LOA, alerting-service coordination rests with the facility serving the FIR or CTA within which the aircraft was flying at last air-ground contact, or which the aircraft was about to enter if last contact was at or close to the boundary. The responsible ACC serves as the control point for collecting information, forwarding it to the RCC, and coordinating with other facilities, and it notifies the listed agencies in the applicable emergency phases.

choices.text: Have the responsible FIR/CTA facility coordinate the alerting service criteria, with the ACC acting as the control point to collect information, notify the RCC and other required parties, and coordinate with affected facilities.
Shift responsibility automatically to the destination FIR the moment destination is known, because boundary and last-contact rules no longer matter once the route is established.
Wait to notify the RCC until the concerned aircraft actually lands or fuel exhaustion is confirmed, because only DETRESFA requires outside notification.
Let each ACC notify only its own internal watch desk, because the section does not assign a single control point for the emergency.

content.explanation: Per 10-6-3, the responsible FIR/CTA facility coordinates the alerting service unless otherwise provided by LOA, and the responsible ACC is the control point for information collection, RCC forwarding, and interfacility coordination.

The responsible ACC serves as the control point for collecting information, forwarding it to the RCC, and coordinating with other facilities, and notifies the listed agencies in the applicable emergency phases.

Shift responsibility automatically to the destination FIR the moment destination is known, because boundary and last-contact rules no longer matter once the route is established.
Wait to notify the RCC until the concerned aircraft actually lands or fuel exhaustion is confirmed, because only DETRESFA requires outside notification.
Let each ACC notify only its own internal watch desk, because the section does not assign a single control point for the emergency.
